docs.title: CareFlow Kids & CareView Kids Documentation
docs.text:
This site documents:

  • CareFlow Kids (Doctor App): clinical workflows, visits, reports, exports
  • CareView Kids (Patient App): opening bundles, viewing reports, growth, documents
  • .pemr bundles: portable patient record format

Start here: - System Overview - CareFlow Kids (Doctor App) - CareView Kids (Patient App)

docs.title: CareFlow Kids / CareView Kids System Map
docs.text: This document explains how the system components interact.

docs.title: CareFlow Kids (Doctor App)
docs.text:
Main clinical application used by physicians.

Capabilities:

  • patient management
  • sick visits
  • well visits
  • growth monitoring
  • AI clinical assistance
  • patient bundle export

Documentation: - Doctor App Guide

docs.title: CareView Kids (Patient App)
docs.text:
Viewer application used by parents or patients.

Capabilities:

  • open patient bundle (.pemr)
  • view visit reports
  • view growth charts
  • access documents
  • add notes

Documentation: - Patient Viewer Guide

docs.title: Patient Bundle (.pemr)
docs.text:
Structure:

flowchart TB
  PEMR[.pemr bundle]
  PEMR --> DB[db.sqlite]
  PEMR --> M[manifest.json]
  PEMR --> DOCS[docs/]

Bundles allow:

  • offline sharing
  • long-term patient record portability
  • privacy-preserving data exchange

docs.text:
flowchart LR
  CF[CareFlow Kids] -->|Export .pemr| PEMR[.pemr bundle]

docs.text:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This section explains what the CareFlow Kids / CareView Kids system is, and how the pieces fit together.

docs.title: Architecture diagram
docs.text:
This diagram shows the high-level components and data flow: CareFlow Kids ⇄ .pemr ⇄ CareView Kids.

flowchart LR

  %% --- CareFlow Kids ---
  subgraph CF[CareFlow Kids]

    Doctor[Doctor]

    CreateBundle[Create a new patient bundle]

    ClinicalWork[Create sick & well visits<br>Record vitals<br>Record growth parameters<br>Generate reports<br>View & upload documents]

    AI[Optional AI querying<br>Optional guideline creation for flagging<br>Flagging abnormal vitals for age<br>Growth trend analysis]

    ImportCF[Import existing bundle]
    ExportCF[Export a bundle]

    CreateBundle --> Doctor
    ImportCF --> Doctor

    Doctor --> ClinicalWork

    AI --> ClinicalWork

    ClinicalWork --> ExportCF

  end


  %% --- Portable bundle ---
  subgraph B[.pemr bundle]

    DB[db.sqlite<br>manifest.json<br>docs folder]

  end


  %% --- CareView Kids ---
  subgraph CV[CareView Kids]

    ImportCV[Import existing bundle]

    ParentFeatures[View visits and reports<br>View growth charts<br>View and upload documents<br>Write parent's notes]

    ExportCV[Export a bundle]

    ImportCV --> ParentFeatures

    ParentFeatures --> ExportCV

  end


  %% --- Bundle exchange ---
  ExportCF --> DB
  DB --> ImportCF

  DB --> ImportCV
  ExportCV --> DB

Notes: - The .pemr bundle is the cumulative patient record (structured visits + documents, plus patient-added notes if enabled). Reports are generated on demand from the structured data.

docs.text: This is the fastest path to using CareFlow Kids effectively in daily practice.

docs.title: 0) Select or create the clinician
docs.text:
When CareFlow Kids opens, the first screen is the clinician selection / login screen.

From there you can:

  • select an existing clinician profile
  • create a new clinician if needed
  • enter a password if required

Once the clinician is selected, the main application opens.

More detailed clinician settings can later be adjusted in the Physician profile section.

docs.title: 1) Start with the patient
docs.text:
• Create a new patient, open an existing patient bundle, or import an existing .pemr file.
  • Confirm demographics and identifiers are correct.

docs.title: 2) Create the right visit type
docs.text:
• Sick visit: acute illness episode
  • Well visit: preventive care and development follow-up

docs.title: 3) Document the visit
docs.text:
Typical minimum:

  • history
  • vitals
  • physical exam
  • working diagnosis
  • plan / guidance

docs.title: 4) Generate a report (PDF or DOCX)
docs.text:
Use reports when you need:

  • a print-friendly summary
  • a shareable record for parents
  • a clean clinical note for your own archive

docs.title: 5) Export the patient record as a .pemr bundle
docs.text:
Exporting a .pemr creates a portable, cumulative patient record including:

  • structured visit data
  • attached documents
  • the cumulative patient record needed for later import and update

docs.title: 6) Import updated .pemr bundles when needed
docs.text: If the family adds notes or documents in CareView Kids, they can export an updated .pemr. Import it back into CareFlow Kids to keep the record cumulative.

docs.title: Quick start (parents)
docs.text: This is the fastest path to using CareView Kids with a .pemr file.

docs.title: The core workflow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  P[Receive .pemr] --> O[Open in CareView Kids]
  O --> V[View visits and reports]
  O --> G[View growth charts]
  O --> D[View & add documents]
  O --> N[Add a note]
  O --> X[Export updated .pemr]

docs.title: 1) Open the .pemr file
docs.text:
• Import the latest bundle received in CareView Kids
  • Tap Open bundle (or equivalent) and select the .pemr file.
  • CareView Kids loads the record locally (offline friendly).

docs.title: 2) View the medical record
docs.text:
• Visits / Reports: readable and shareable summaries (PDF)
  • Growth charts: weight / height / head circumference trends
  • Documents: vaccines, labs, imaging, parent-provided files

docs.title: 3) Add a note (optional)
docs.text:
Use notes for:

  • symptoms at home
  • questions for the doctor
  • medication responses
  • “what happened since last visit”

docs.title: 4) Export an updated .pemr (when asked)
docs.text:
If your doctor asks for an updated record:

  • Export a new .pemr
  • Send it back to the clinic
  • The doctor can import it into CareFlow Kids

docs.title: Don’t worry, you can’t “break” the record
docs.text:
• You can always keep the original .pemr as a backup copy.
  • Exporting creates a new .pemr file, so you can keep versions if needed.

docs.title: System map
docs.text: This page shows how CareFlow Kids (doctor app), CareView Kids (patient app), and the .pemr bundle work together.

docs.title: Clinician view (CareFlow Kids ⇄ .pemr ⇄ CareView Kids)
docs.text:
Goal: document care, generate reports, and share a portable patient record.

flowchart LR
  CF[CareFlow Kids]
  PEMR[.pemr bundle]
  CV[CareView Kids]
  R[Reports]
  D[Documents]

  CF -->|Export .pemr| PEMR
  PEMR -->|Import .pemr| CF

  PEMR -->|Open .pemr| CV
  CV -->|Export updated .pemr| PEMR
  CF -->|Generate reports| R
  CF -->|Attach documents| D
  R --> PEMR
  D --> PEMR

Key idea: the .pemr bundle is cumulative — it carries the patient’s longitudinal record (visits + reports + documents, plus patient-added notes if enabled).

What the clinician does: - Record sick visits + well visits - Generate reports (PDF) - Attach documents (e.g., vaccine records, labs) - Export a .pemr bundle for the family - Import an updated .pemr bundle from the family when needed

docs.title: Parent view (open bundle, read, add note)
docs.text:
Goal: view the child’s record offline and keep documents together.

flowchart LR
  PEMR[.pemr bundle]
  CV[CareView Kids]
  V[View & share visits reports]
  G[View growth charts]
  DOC[Add & View documents]
  N[Add a note]

  PEMR -->|Open .pemr| CV
  CV --> V
  CV --> G
  CV --> DOC
  CV --> N

  CV -->|Export updated .pemr| PEMR

What the parent can do: - Read reports and visit summaries - View growth charts - Open & store documents - Add notes (kept with the bundle)

docs.title: What’s inside a .pemr bundle
docs.text:
At a high level:

  • db.sqlite — structured visit data
  • manifest.json — metadata and versioning
  • docs/ — uploaded documents (PDF/images)

The bundle is designed to be portable, local-first, and shareable.

docs.title: Clinical terminology (SNOMED)
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids uses a subset of SNOMED CT terminology to normalize clinical data recorded during consultations.

The terminology layer allows structured clinical information to be interpreted consistently by:

  • the guideline rule engine
  • the AI assistant
  • reporting and data export tools

docs.title: SNOMED subset used in CareFlow Kids
docs.text:
The application does not include the full SNOMED CT terminology.

Instead, CareFlow Kids uses a restricted subset of SNOMED concepts corresponding to the structured fields used in the consultation interface.

Examples include:

  • symptoms
  • physical examination findings
  • clinical conditions
  • relevant contextual factors

This subset is mapped to the structured UI elements used during sick visits.

The purpose of this approach is to:

  • keep the system lightweight
  • simplify guideline rule creation
  • maintain consistent terminology across the application

docs.title: Terminology flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Structured UI fields] --> B[Normalized clinical tokens]
    B --> C[SNOMED subset concepts]
    C --> D[Guideline rule engine]
    C --> E[AI request builder]
    C --> F[Problem listing generator]

This diagram shows how structured clinical information is transformed into normalized concepts that can be reused consistently across the application.

docs.title: Role of SNOMED normalization
docs.text:
During a consultation:

  1. Clinical data is entered through the structured UI.
  2. These inputs are mapped to normalized clinical tokens.
  3. These tokens correspond to SNOMED concepts.

These normalized tokens are then used by:

  • the guideline rule engine
  • the AI request builder
  • the problem listing generator

This ensures that different expressions of the same clinical concept can be interpreted consistently.

docs.title: Licensing note
docs.text:
SNOMED CT is a licensed terminology system managed by SNOMED International.

Use and distribution of SNOMED terminology is subject to the licensing rules defined by SNOMED International and by national member organizations.

CareFlow Kids does not redistribute the full SNOMED CT dataset.

Instead, the system relies on a limited subset of clinical terms mapped internally to SNOMED identifiers.

Users or developers distributing software based on SNOMED terminology should ensure compliance with the licensing requirements applicable in their jurisdiction.

More information is available at:

https://www.snomed.org

docs.title: Documents and manifest
docs.text:
This page explains how documents and metadata are stored inside a .pemr bundle.

Together, the docs/ folder and the manifest.json file allow applications to manage attached files safely and consistently.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle contains both structured clinical data and external documents.

The structured medical data is stored in the encrypted database (db.sqlite), while files such as PDFs or images are stored separately in the docs/ folder.

Metadata describing the bundle and its contents is stored in manifest.json.

flowchart TB
  PEMR[.pemr bundle]

  PEMR --> DB[db.sqlite]
  PEMR --> MANIFEST[manifest.json]
  PEMR --> DOCS[docs/]

  DOCS --> FILES[PDF / Images / Documents]

docs.title: The docs/ folder
docs.text:
The docs/ directory stores documents attached to the patient record.

Typical examples include:

  • laboratory reports
  • vaccination records
  • imaging reports
  • discharge summaries
  • documents provided by the family

Documents are stored as normal files (for example .pdf, .jpg, .png).

docs.title: Why documents are stored outside the database
docs.text:
Documents are not stored inside db.sqlite for several reasons:

  • large binary files would make the database inefficient
  • files can be opened directly by the operating system
  • documents can be inspected if the bundle is unpacked

If the .pemr bundle is unpacked (for example by unzipping it), the docs/ folder appears as a normal directory containing those files.

docs.title: The manifest.json file
docs.text:
The manifest.json file provides metadata describing the bundle.

Applications use this file to verify compatibility and understand how to process the bundle.

Typical information stored in the manifest includes:

  • bundle format version
  • schema version
  • creation timestamp
  • application version
  • identifiers for included resources

docs.title: Example structure
docs.text:
{
  "bundleVersion": "1",
  "schemaVersion": "1",
  "createdAt": "2026-03-04T10:12:00Z",
  "createdBy": "CareFlow Kids",
  "appVersion": "1.2.0"
}

The exact fields may evolve as the bundle format evolves.

docs.title: How applications use the manifest
docs.text:
When opening or importing a .pemr bundle, an application typically:

  1. reads manifest.json
  2. verifies bundle version compatibility
  3. verifies database integrity
  4. loads the encrypted db.sqlite
  5. indexes documents found in docs/

This process ensures that the bundle can be safely opened even as the software evolves.

docs.title: Relationship between database and documents
docs.text:
The database (db.sqlite) stores references to documents, not the files themselves.

For example, a visit record in the database may contain a reference to:

docs/lab-report-2026-03-01.pdf

The application then resolves that path to the corresponding file in the docs/ folder.

This design keeps the bundle:

  • efficient
  • easy to inspect
  • robust when files are added or updated

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Inside a .pemr bundle:

  • db.sqlite contains structured clinical data
  • docs/ contains attached documents
  • manifest.json describes the bundle and ensures compatibility

Together these components make the bundle portable, inspectable, and safe to exchange between applications.

docs.title: .pemr bundle
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle is the portable patient record used by the CareFlow Kids / CareView Kids ecosystem.

It allows a patient's medical information to move safely between the doctor application and the patient viewer, while remaining usable offline.

docs.title: Core idea
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  CF[CareFlow Kids<br>Doctor App]
  PEMR[.pemr bundle<br>Portable patient record]
  CV[CareView Kids<br>Patient App]

  CF -->|Export| PEMR
  PEMR -->|Open| CV
  CV -->|Export updated| PEMR
  PEMR -->|Import| CF

The .pemr bundle is the central data container that carries the patient's record between applications.

docs.title: What a .pemr bundle contains
docs.text:
At a high level, the bundle contains three main components:

flowchart TB
  PEMR[.pemr bundle]

  PEMR --> DB[db.sqlite]
  PEMR --> MANIFEST[manifest.json]
  PEMR --> DOCS[docs/]

docs.title: db.sqlite
docs.text:
The SQLite database storing structured clinical information:

  • patient demographics
  • visits (sick visits and well visits)
  • vitals
  • growth measurements
  • notes
  • clinical observations

docs.title: Database security
docs.text:
The db.sqlite file is encrypted.

The encryption uses HMAC / SHA-256 based protection to ensure integrity and confidentiality of the structured medical data stored in the database. Applications opening the bundle are responsible for performing the necessary verification and decryption before accessing the data.

docs.title: Security properties of the bundle
docs.text:
The .pemr format is designed with several security properties:

  • Database encryption — the structured clinical data stored in db.sqlite is encrypted.
  • Integrity verification — HMAC / SHA‑256 mechanisms allow applications to verify that the database has not been altered.
  • Manifest validation — the manifest.json file allows applications to verify bundle version and compatibility before import.
  • Separation of data and documents — attached files remain in the docs/ folder and can be inspected independently of the database.

These properties help ensure that patient data remains confidential, portable, and verifiable when bundles are exchanged between applications.

docs.title: manifest.json
docs.text:
Metadata describing the bundle.

Typical fields include:

  • bundle version
  • schema version
  • creation timestamp
  • application version

The manifest allows applications to check compatibility before importing a bundle.

docs.title: docs/
docs.text:
A folder containing attached documents such as:

  • laboratory reports
  • vaccination records
  • imaging reports
  • parent-provided documents

Documents remain separate files so they can be viewed easily without opening the database. If the .pemr bundle is unpacked (for example by unzipping it), the files in the docs/ folder can be accessed directly like normal documents.

docs.title: Why bundles are used
docs.text:
The .pemr format enables:

  • portable patient records
  • offline-first operation
  • privacy-preserving data exchange

Because the bundle is self-contained, it can be shared:

  • between clinic and family
  • between clinics
  • for long-term archival

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  V[Doctor visit]
  R[Report generated]
  E[Export .pemr]
  F[Family opens in CareView]
  U[Family adds notes or documents]
  X[Export updated .pemr]
  I[Doctor imports bundle]

  V --> R --> E --> F --> U --> X --> I

This workflow keeps the patient's record cumulative and continuously updated.

docs.title: File extension
docs.text:
The bundle uses the extension:

.pemr

The extension identifies a Portable Electronic Medical Record bundle used by CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle is:

  • a portable patient record
  • a self-contained medical dataset
  • the data bridge between doctor and patient applications

Understanding the bundle concept is key to understanding how the entire system works.

docs.title: Versioning and compatibility
docs.text:
The .pemr format is designed to remain stable over time while allowing the applications to evolve.

Versioning ensures that bundles created by one version of the software can still be safely opened by newer versions.

docs.title: Why versioning is necessary
docs.text:
Medical records may exist for many years. During that time:

  • the applications may evolve
  • the database schema may change
  • new features may appear

Without versioning, an older bundle could become unreadable.

The .pemr format therefore includes explicit version metadata that allows applications to determine how a bundle should be handled.

docs.title: Where version information is stored
docs.text:
Version information is stored in manifest.json.

Typical fields include:

{
  "bundleVersion": "1",
  "schemaVersion": "1",
  "appVersion": "1.2.0"
}

docs.title: bundleVersion
docs.text:
The bundle version defines the structure of the .pemr format itself.

This includes things like:

  • directory structure
  • required files
  • bundle conventions

docs.title: schemaVersion
docs.text:
The schema version defines the structure of the database (db.sqlite).

It indicates how tables and fields are organized.

docs.title: appVersion
docs.text:
The application version indicates which version of the software created the bundle.

This is useful for debugging and migration logic.

docs.title: Compatibility policy
docs.text: CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids follow a simple compatibility policy.

docs.title: Forward compatibility
docs.text:
Newer versions of the applications should be able to open older bundles.

If a schema update is required, the application performs a migration of the database.

docs.title: Backward compatibility
docs.text:
Older applications versions may not be able to open bundles created by newer versions.

If the bundle format or schema is too new, the application will refuse the import and display an error message.

docs.title: Migration process
docs.text:
When a bundle with an older schema is opened:

  1. The application reads manifest.json
  2. It checks the schemaVersion
  3. If needed, it runs a database migration
  4. The schema is updated to the current version

This ensures that older records remain usable.

docs.title: Stability principle
docs.text:
The .pemr format is designed with a stability-first philosophy:

  • changes should be rare and deliberate
  • migrations should be safe and reversible
  • older records should remain usable for many years

Maintaining long-term access to patient data is a core design goal of the system.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Versioning ensures that:

  • .pemr bundles remain usable over time
  • database schemas can evolve safely
  • applications can detect incompatible bundles

This mechanism allows the ecosystem to evolve while preserving the long-term integrity of medical records.

docs.title: Visit types
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids organizes medical encounters into two main visit types.

This distinction keeps the clinical record clear and reflects how pediatric care is typically structured.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  PATIENT[Patient]

  PATIENT --> SICK[Sick visit]
  PATIENT --> WELL[Well visit]

Every patient can have multiple visits of both types over time.

docs.title: Sick visits
docs.text:
A sick visit represents an episode of illness or a medical concern requiring evaluation.

Typical examples include:

  • fever
  • respiratory infection
  • rash
  • abdominal pain
  • injury

docs.title: Typical content
docs.text:
A sick visit usually includes:

  • history of present illness
  • vital signs
  • physical examination
  • working diagnosis
  • treatment plan or guidance

docs.title: Clinical goal
docs.text:
The goal of a sick visit is to evaluate and manage an acute problem.

Each sick visit represents a clinical episode.

docs.title: Well visits
docs.text:
A well visit is a preventive consultation focusing on growth, development, and routine care.

Typical examples include:

  • newborn follow‑up
  • scheduled pediatric checkups
  • vaccination visits
  • developmental evaluations

docs.title: Typical content
docs.text:
Well visits often include:

  • growth measurements
  • developmental milestone review
  • feeding and nutrition discussion
  • vaccination review
  • anticipatory guidance for parents

docs.title: Clinical goal
docs.text: The goal of a well visit is to monitor healthy development and prevent disease.

docs.title: Relationship with reports
docs.text:
Both visit types can generate reports.

flowchart LR
  VISIT[Visit]
  REPORT[PDF report]

  VISIT --> REPORT

Reports provide a summary of the visit that can be:

  • printed
  • shared
  • archived

The information used to generate reports is stored in the database (db.sqlite), not as static PDF files.

When a user requests a report, the application generates the PDF (or DOCX) on the fly from the structured visit data.

This design keeps the .pemr bundle very lightweight, even for patients with many visits, because the bundle stores only the structured clinical data rather than pre‑generated documents.

Both CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids generate reports dynamically from the database when needed.

docs.title: Visit timeline
docs.text:
Over time, a patient's medical history becomes a sequence of visits.

flowchart LR
  V1[Sick visit]
  V2[Well visit]
  V3[Sick visit]
  V4[Well visit]

  V1 --> V2 --> V3 --> V4

This timeline forms the longitudinal medical record of the patient.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids distinguishes two visit types:

  • Sick visits — evaluation and management of acute problems
  • Well visits — preventive care and developmental monitoring

Both contribute to the long‑term medical record and may generate reports that can be shared with families.

docs.title: Ask AI
docs.text:
The Ask AI section explains how to configure and use the AI assistant in CareFlow Kids.

Unlike the AI clinical support page inside the Sick Visit documentation (which explains how AI is used during a consultation), this section explains how the AI system itself is configured and controlled by the physician.

AI integration in CareFlow Kids follows three main principles:

  • Physician‑controlled configuration
  • Structured clinical input (JSON)
  • Clear separation between local guideline rules and external AI analysis

AI is optional and can be disabled simply by leaving the configuration empty.

docs.title: AI architecture overview
docs.text:
The AI system operates using the following pipeline:

flowchart LR
    A[Structured visit data] --> B[Episode JSON snapshot]
    B --> C[Physician prompt template]
    C --> D[Online AI provider]
    D --> E[Structured AI answer]

The physician controls both:

  • which AI provider is used
  • how the AI is instructed to answer (prompt rules)

docs.title: AI providers
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids supports external AI providers.

Typical configuration includes:

  • API endpoint (for example https://api.openai.com/v1)
  • Model name (for example gpt‑5‑mini)
  • Provider selection (OpenAI or compatible provider)
  • API key

These settings are configured in the Physician profile → AI assistant section.

The API key is stored locally and used only when the physician explicitly requests AI assistance.

No AI request is sent automatically.

Even when AI is configured, the system only sends clinical data to the AI provider when the physician explicitly clicks Ask AI. If the feature is not used, no data is transmitted.

docs.title: Prompt configuration
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids allows the physician to define custom prompt templates.

Prompts control:

  • how the AI interprets clinical data
  • what sources it should rely on
  • the format of the response

The physician defines how the AI assistant behaves using a configurable prompt template available in the Physician profile section of the application.

Typical prompt instructions include:

  • using trusted pediatric medical sources
  • structuring answers in a predictable format
  • highlighting evidence quality
  • generating coding suggestions when appropriate

Prompts can also instruct the AI to generate useful clinical outputs such as:

  • structured visit summaries
  • parent information handouts
  • medical certificates
  • patient instructions or care guidance

Because the AI receives structured clinical information, these outputs can be generated in a consistent and clinically relevant format.

Because prompts are physician‑editable, the AI assistant can be adapted to different practice styles.

docs.title: JSON preview
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids generates a structured JSON representation of the episode before sending an AI request. JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a standardized text format used to organize structured data.

The JSON preview feature allows the physician to inspect the exact information that will be transmitted to the AI provider.

This improves transparency and makes debugging AI behavior easier.

The JSON typically contains:

  • patient age and sex
  • symptoms and complaints
  • abnormal vital signs
  • examination findings
  • problem listing
  • relevant contextual information
  • relevant patient history (for example perinatal history or past medical history when available)

Direct identifiers such as name, alias, or MRN are never included.

docs.title: Advanced usage
docs.text:
Advanced users may adapt the AI configuration to suit specific workflows.

Examples include:

  • refining prompts to emphasize certain medical sources
  • adjusting response structure
  • customizing coding instructions
  • experimenting with different AI models

Because the AI assistant always receives structured clinical data, prompt changes can significantly influence the quality and usefulness of the responses.

docs.title: Clinical responsibility
docs.text:
AI responses are advisory only.

They may help with:

  • summarizing information
  • exploring differential diagnoses
  • suggesting investigations

However, clinical decisions remain the responsibility of the physician.

The AI assistant is designed to support — not replace — clinical judgement.

docs.title: Creating Guidelines
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids supports internal clinical guideline creation.

Guidelines are structured logic rules that allow the system to analyze clinical information entered during a consultation.

They can be used to:

  • interpret symptoms
  • detect red flags
  • match clinical criteria
  • suggest investigations
  • suggest differential diagnoses

Guidelines run locally inside the application and do not require AI or a connection to the internet.

docs.title: How guideline matching works
docs.text:
During a consultation, the clinician enters information through the structured visit interface.

The system continuously evaluates this information against the configured guideline rules.

flowchart LR
    A[Structured clinical data entered in UI]
    B[Normalized clinical tokens<br>SNOMED concepts + structured keys]
    C[Guideline rule engine]
    D[Matched guideline flags]
    E[Clinical suggestions<br>or alerts]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

The process works as follows:

  1. Clinical information is entered in the visit form.
  2. The system converts this information into normalized tokens.
  3. Guideline rules evaluate the tokens.
  4. When rule conditions match, a flag is generated.
  5. The flag can produce a clinical suggestion or alert.

docs.title: Normalizing clinical information
docs.text:
To ensure reliable matching, CareFlow Kids uses SNOMED CT concepts alongside structured field keys.

This allows clinical findings to be represented in a standardized way.

Examples include:

  • structured keys (for example vital.temp_c.max)
  • symptom flags (for example symptom.fever.present)
  • SNOMED CT identifiers (for example sct:56018004)

Using standardized concepts ensures that guidelines remain robust and reusable.

docs.title: Structure of a guideline rule
docs.text:
A guideline is defined as a set of logical conditions.

Each rule typically contains:

  • a flag name (the label shown when the rule matches)
  • a priority (importance level). When multiple rules match, higher priority values are evaluated and shown first.
  • one or more conditions

Conditions may use logical operators such as:

  • all (all conditions must match)
  • any (at least one condition must match)

Example structure:

{
  "flag": "Febrile Child Red Flags",
  "priority": 95,
  "when": {
    "all": [
      {"key": "symptom.fever.present", "op": "present"},
      {"key": "vital.temp_c.max", "op": "gte", "value_number": 38}
    ]
  }
}

When the conditions are satisfied, the guideline rule produces a flag.

If several guideline rules match at the same time, the priority value determines which guideline is displayed first. Higher priority values indicate more important clinical rules, such as red‑flag conditions or urgent safety alerts.

Example priority levels:

  • priority 95 → critical safety rule (for example sepsis or meningitis red flags)
  • priority 50 → important guideline recommendation
  • priority 10 → informational or supportive clinical flag

docs.title: Using the guideline builder
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids provides a visual guideline builder to simplify rule creation.

This interface allows the clinician to:

  • create or edit guideline rules
  • add logical conditions
  • select standardized keys
  • insert SNOMED CT concepts
  • adjust rule priority

The visual builder automatically generates the corresponding JSON rule.

docs.title: Exporting and sharing guidelines
docs.text:
Guideline rules are stored as JSON.

They can be:

  • exported
  • shared
  • reused in other installations of CareFlow Kids

This makes it possible to build shared clinical rule libraries.

docs.title: Relationship with AI
docs.text:
Guideline rules operate independently from the AI assistant.

They run locally and provide:

  • guideline-based alerts
  • clinical suggestions
  • structured flags

These flags can then be used to inform AI analysis, but they do not require AI to function.

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Documents
docs.text:
Documents attached to a patient are stored in the bundle /docs folder.

Examples include:

  • Vaccination records
  • Lab reports
  • Imaging results
  • Parent-provided documents

These documents travel with the patient bundle.

docs.title: Documents
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids allows clinicians to attach external medical documents to a patient record.

These documents are stored directly inside the patient bundle in the /docs folder.

Examples include:

  • vaccination records
  • laboratory reports
  • imaging results
  • discharge summaries
  • parent‑provided documents

Documents are stored as normal files so they can be easily opened outside the application if needed.

docs.title: Where documents are stored
docs.text:
Every patient bundle (.pemr) contains a document folder:

.pemr
├── db.sqlite
├── manifest.json
└── docs/

The docs/ folder contains all uploaded files associated with the patient.

This design ensures that documents travel with the patient bundle when it is exported or shared.

docs.title: Adding a document
docs.text:
To attach a document to a patient:

  1. Open the patient bundle.
  2. From the main dashboard, click the Documents button.
  3. In the Documents panel, click Upload document.
  4. Select the file from your computer.

Documents should always be added through this dedicated interface. Files should not be manually inserted into the bundle by opening the .pemr file.

docs.title: Viewing documents
docs.text:
Uploaded documents can be opened directly from the Documents panel.

Typical uses include:

  • reviewing laboratory results
  • checking vaccination records
  • accessing imaging reports

Because documents remain normal files, they can also be opened by the operating system's default viewer.

Although documents can technically be accessed by unzipping a .pemr bundle and opening the docs/ folder, this should be considered a last resort. Directly modifying the contents of the bundle outside the application may break the bundle's integrity. Documents should normally be accessed through CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids.

docs.title: Deleting a document
docs.text:
If a document was uploaded by mistake, it can be removed from the document list.

Deleting a document removes the file from the docs/ folder of the patient bundle.

docs.title: Documents and patient bundles
docs.text:
When a patient bundle is exported:

  • the database (db.sqlite)
  • the manifest (manifest.json)
  • the documents folder (docs/)

are all included in the .pemr file.

This ensures that clinical data and attached files always remain together.

docs.title: Documents in CareView Kids
docs.text:
Documents stored in the bundle are also visible in CareView Kids, allowing parents or patients to:

  • view uploaded files
  • access reports
  • keep important medical documents together with the record

Parents or patients may also add documents from CareView Kids. When a bundle is later imported into CareFlow Kids, these documents become available to the clinician in the Documents panel, allowing families to contribute relevant medical information between consultations.

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Exporting Patient Bundles
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids can export a full patient record as a .pemr bundle.

A .pemr bundle is a portable patient record that contains the structured medical information, attached documents, and metadata needed to reconstruct the record in another application.

Bundles allow the patient record to move safely between:

  • CareFlow Kids (doctor application)
  • CareView Kids (patient / family viewer)

This design supports a portable, patient‑controlled medical record.

docs.title: When to export a bundle
docs.text:
Exporting a bundle is typically done:

  • after a consultation
  • when the record has been updated
  • when the family needs the latest version of the patient file

Clinicians are encouraged to export the updated bundle before removing the patient from the active workspace.

docs.title: How to export a bundle
docs.text:
1. Open the patient bundle in CareFlow Kids.
  2. Go to the Patient dashboard.
  3. Click the Export bundle button.
  4. Choose a location to save the file.

CareFlow Kids then generates a .pemr file containing the full patient record.

docs.title: Contents of the bundle
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle contains the following structure:

.pemr
├── db.sqlite
├── manifest.json
└── docs/
  • db.sqlite → encrypted structured medical record
  • manifest.json → bundle metadata and integrity information
  • docs/ → attached documents (labs, imaging, vaccination records, etc.)

docs.title: Reports inside the bundle
docs.text:
Visit reports are not stored as PDF files inside the bundle.

Instead, the bundle stores the structured clinical data used to generate reports.

When a report is requested:

  • CareFlow Kids
  • or CareView Kids

can generate the report on demand from the database.

This keeps .pemr files very lightweight, even for patients with many visits.

docs.title: Using the bundle in CareView Kids
docs.text:
Families can open the .pemr file in CareView Kids to:

  • view visit summaries
  • view growth charts
  • access documents
  • add notes or documents

When the family exports an updated bundle from CareView Kids, it can later be re‑imported into CareFlow Kids to update the clinical record.

docs.title: Keeping a backup of the patient bundle
docs.text:
Families are encouraged to keep a backup copy of the .pemr file in a safe place.

Typical locations include:

  • a computer
  • a phone or tablet
  • a secure cloud storage location

Keeping a backup ensures that the patient record can always be recovered if a device is lost or replaced.

The .pemr file is the complete portable medical record, so preserving a copy is recommended.

docs.title: Patient record exchange workflow
docs.text:
The .pemr bundle allows the patient record to circulate between the doctor and the family while remaining a single cumulative record.

flowchart LR
    A[CareFlow Kids<br>Doctor consultation] --> B[Export .pemr bundle]
    B --> C[Open in CareView Kids]
    C --> D[View reports / growth charts<br>Add notes or documents]
    D --> E[Export updated .pemr]
    E --> F[Import into CareFlow Kids<br>Next consultation]
    F --> B

This loop allows the patient record to remain continuously updated while staying portable.

docs.title: Important safety note
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle should normally be handled only through the application interface.

Although it is technically possible to unzip the bundle and inspect its contents, modifying the internal files manually may break the integrity of the record.

Always use CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids to modify bundle contents.

docs.title: Growth Charts
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids generates WHO‑based pediatric growth charts from the measurements recorded during visits.

Growth charts help clinicians evaluate whether a child's growth pattern is normal, accelerated, or concerning for their age and sex.

The system automatically compares measurements against WHO reference standards.

docs.title: Measurements used
docs.text:
Growth charts are generated from measurements recorded during visits:

  • Weight
  • Height / Length
  • Head circumference
  • BMI / Weight‑for‑length

These measurements are recorded in the Vitals section of the visit form.

Multiple measurements over time allow the system to visualize growth trends.

Growth measurements can be added in two ways:

  • Manually through the Growth Table in the Growth section of the dashboard.
  • Automatically from vitals recorded during visits, including sick visits where weight or other measurements are entered.

The Growth Table therefore serves as the central historical record of all growth measurements for the patient.

docs.title: Growth standards
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids uses WHO pediatric growth references.

These standards provide:

  • percentile curves
  • z‑score calculations
  • age‑adjusted reference values

The charts are generated separately for:

  • boys
  • girls

This ensures accurate comparison with the appropriate population reference.

docs.title: Chart features
docs.text:
Growth charts in CareFlow Kids include:

  • Percentile curves (WHO reference curves)
  • Z‑score calculation for each measurement
  • Growth trend visualization across visits

This allows clinicians to quickly identify:

  • growth acceleration
  • growth deceleration
  • possible nutritional or medical concerns
flowchart LR
    A[Growth measurements<br>Weight / Height / HC / BMI]
    B[Growth table history<br>All recorded measurements]
    C[WHO reference standards]
    D[Percentiles & Z-scores]
    E[Growth charts & trend analysis]

    A --> B
    B --> D
    C --> D
    D --> E

docs.title: Accessing growth charts
docs.text:
Growth charts can be accessed from the Growth section of the patient dashboard.

The interface allows clinicians to:

  • display weight‑for‑age charts
  • display length/height‑for‑age charts
  • display head‑circumference charts
  • display BMI or weight‑for‑length charts

Charts automatically include all measurements recorded for the patient.

docs.title: Adding growth measurements
docs.text:
Growth data can be entered manually through the Growth Table interface.

This allows clinicians to add measurements collected:

  • during well visits
  • during external consultations
  • from parental records or health booklets

For well visits, it is recommended to record the measurements close to the expected visit date, so that the growth evaluation performed during that visit reflects the most accurate data.

docs.title: Growth charts in reports
docs.text:
When a well visit report is generated, the relevant growth charts are included in the report.

This allows families to easily visualize the child's growth trajectory.

During well visits, CareFlow Kids also performs a growth trend analysis based on the available measurements.

This analysis may include:

  • short‑term weight gain evaluation for young infants
  • long‑term growth trajectory assessment for older children

These evaluations help clinicians identify deviations from expected growth patterns early.

docs.title: Growth charts in CareView Kids
docs.text:
When the .pemr bundle is opened in CareView Kids, parents can also view the growth charts.

This allows families to:

  • track the child's growth between visits
  • review charts discussed during consultations

The charts displayed in CareView Kids use the same data and reference standards as CareFlow Kids.

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Importing a .pemr bundle
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids can import an existing .pemr bundle to restore or update a patient record.

Importing allows the clinic to receive updates from families using CareView Kids and to continue working on the same cumulative portable medical record.

docs.title: Typical use cases
docs.text:
Importing a .pemr is useful when:

  • a family sends back an updated record with notes or documents
  • a patient returns for a follow-up consultation with their existing bundle
  • a previously exported patient file needs to be restored into the app
  • another clinic using the same system shares the patient record

docs.title: How to import a .pemr
docs.text:
1. Open CareFlow Kids.
  2. Go to Patient management.
  3. Click the Import bundle button.
  4. Choose the .pemr file.
  5. Confirm the import.

After import, the patient bundle becomes available in the app and appears in the sidebar / recent bundles list.

Screenshot

docs.title: What happens during import
docs.text:
During import, CareFlow Kids will:

  • read the bundle manifest
  • verify the bundle format
  • verify schema compatibility
  • load the encrypted patient database
  • attach the documents contained in the bundle
  • restore the patient record into the app workspace

The imported bundle can then be opened normally for viewing, editing, and follow-up documentation.

docs.title: Safety checks
docs.text:
The application verifies several elements before completing the import:

  • bundle format
  • schema version compatibility
  • database integrity
  • document integrity

If a problem is detected, the import stops and an error message is displayed.

docs.title: After import
docs.text:
Once imported, the patient record becomes available in CareFlow Kids and can be used normally.

Typical next steps include:

  • opening the patient record from the sidebar
  • reviewing previous visits
  • creating a new sick visit or well visit
  • generating reports on demand
  • exporting the updated .pemr after the consultation

docs.title: Relationship with the .pemr loop
docs.text:
Import is one half of the portable patient record loop:

flowchart LR
    A[CareFlow Kids<br>Doctor consultation] --> B[Export .pemr]
    B --> C[Open in CareView Kids]
    C --> D[Add notes / documents<br>View reports and charts]
    D --> E[Export updated .pemr]
    E --> F[Import into CareFlow Kids]
    F --> A

This loop allows the same patient record to circulate between the clinic and the family while remaining cumulative and portable.

docs.title: Important note
docs.text:
Importing a .pemr restores the patient bundle into the application workspace, but the portable bundle itself remains the authoritative patient file.

After making changes in CareFlow Kids, clinicians should export the updated and parent import the updated .pemr so the portable record stays current.

docs.title: Patient Management
docs.text: This section explains how to create, search, and manage patients in CareFlow Kids.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Create a new patient
  • Search or open an existing patient
  • Patient demographics
  • Patient identifiers (MRN)

docs.title: Create a new patient
docs.text: This page explains the recommended workflow for creating a patient in CareFlow Kids.

docs.title: When to create a new patient
docs.text:
Create a new patient when: - the child has never been seen in your CareFlow Kids database - you are starting a record for a newborn or a newly registered family

If the family provides an existing .pemr file, prefer Import instead of creating a new patient (so the record stays cumulative).

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1) Open Patient management

2) Select Create new patient

3) Enter the minimum required fields Recommended minimum: - family name - given name - date of birth - sex

4) Confirm identifiers - The app auto-generates an MRN, verify it was assigned. - The app auto-generate an alias

5) Save the patient

docs.title: After creation: what to do next
docs.text: Typically: - open the patient record - create the visit (Sick visit or Well visit) - document findings and vitals - generate a report when needed - export and share a .pemr when you want the family to have the updated record

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Patient demographics
docs.text:
Patient demographics describe the basic identifying characteristics of the patient.

These fields help ensure that the correct patient record is used during consultations and that medical information is associated with the right individual.

Patient demographic information is stored inside the **.pemr bundledatabase (db.sqlite`).

docs.title: Core demographic fields
docs.text:
Typical demographic information includes:

  • family name
  • given name
  • date of birth
  • sex

These fields are required because they are essential for:

  • identifying the patient
  • calculating the patient's age
  • displaying correct information in reports

docs.title: Age calculation
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids automatically calculates the patient's age based on the date of birth.

Age is used in many clinical contexts, including:

  • pediatric growth charts
  • visit summaries
  • medical documentation

Because age is derived automatically, the date of birth must be entered carefully.

docs.title: Relationship with visits
docs.text:
Patient demographic information is shared across all visits belonging to that patient.

This means:

  • sick visits
  • well visits
  • reports

all reference the same patient demographic data.

Updating demographic information therefore affects all future documentation generated from the patient record.

docs.title: Demographics and the .pemr bundle
docs.text:
Demographic data is part of the patient's portable record.

When a .pemr bundle is exported:

  • the demographic information travels with the bundle
  • the receiving application restores the patient identity

This ensures that the medical record remains consistent across applications.

docs.title: Practical guidance
docs.text:
When creating or editing a patient:

  • verify spelling of the family and given name
  • verify the date of birth carefully
  • confirm the sex recorded in the file

Accurate demographic information helps prevent patient identification errors.

docs.title: Patient identifiers (MRN and alias)
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids uses two automatic identifiers for each patient:

  • MRN (Medical Record Number)
  • Alias (human-friendly bundle label)

Both are generated automatically and are not editable.

docs.title: MRN (stable clinical identifier)
docs.text:
The MRN is the primary identifier intended for clinical record keeping.

Properties:

  • unique per patient
  • stable over time
  • used for internal linking and database integrity
  • displayed for reference

docs.title: Alias (practical bundle identifier)
docs.text:
The alias is a practical label used to help clinicians quickly recognize the patient bundle in the sidebar.

Properties:

  • auto-generated
  • designed to be memorable and visually distinct
  • used as the main label for loaded bundles

docs.title: Where you see MRN and alias
docs.text:
In Patient management:

  • The alias is shown prominently in the sidebar list of loaded bundles.
  • The MRN is available in the patient details view.

docs.title: Relationship with .pemr bundles
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle represents a single patient record.

  • The MRN and alias belong to that patient record.
  • When you export a .pemr, those identifiers remain part of the record.
  • When you import a .pemr, CareFlow Kids restores the patient record and its identifiers.

docs.title: Practical guidance
docs.text:
• Use the alias to quickly select the correct loaded patient bundle.
  • Use the MRN when you need a stable identifier for clinical workflows or cross-checking.

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Search or open an existing patient
docs.text:
This page explains how to quickly find and open a patient record in CareFlow Kids.

In daily use, this is usually the most common entry point.

docs.title: Before you create a new patient
docs.text:
Always search first to avoid duplicates.

Create a new patient only if:

  • the child is truly not in your database, and
  • the family does not provide an existing .pemr file.

If the family provides a .pemr, use Import instead (so the record stays cumulative).

docs.title: How to access a patient
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids does not maintain a large searchable patient database.

Instead, the application works with individual patient bundles (.pemr) that are temporarily loaded into the app.

Patient bundles appear in the left sidebar under Recent bundles.

Typical ways to access a patient:

docs.title: 1) Import an existing patient bundle
docs.text:
Use the Import icon (top left).

This loads a .pemr bundle provided by the family or another clinic.

Once imported, the patient appears in the sidebar and can be opened.

docs.title: 2) Create a new patient
docs.text:
Use the Create patient icon (person with +, above the sidebar).

This creates a new patient bundle that becomes available in the sidebar.

docs.title: 3) Open a loaded patient
docs.text:
Patient bundles currently present in the application appear in the sidebar.

Select the patient to open the record and begin working with it.

Deleting a patient bundle

Patient bundles can be deleted by swiping them from the sidebar.

However, you should never delete a bundle before exporting it, otherwise the patient's record would be permanently lost.

Recommended workflow:

  1. Update the patient record
  2. Export the updated .pemr bundle
  3. Verify the export
  4. Only then remove the bundle from the app if desired

docs.title: Open the patient record
docs.text:
When the patient appears in results:

  • select the patient
  • open the patient record (or set as active patient)

Once opened, you can:

  • create a sick visit or well visit
  • view previous visits
  • generate a report on demand
  • export a .pemr

docs.title: Importing an existing .pemr
docs.text:
If you receive a patient bundle from the family or another clinic:

  • choose Import from Patient management
  • select the .pemr file

After import, the patient record becomes available in the database and can be opened normally.

docs.title: Physician profile
docs.text:
The Physician Profile section stores the doctor-specific settings used by CareFlow Kids.

This section is important because it controls not only the physician identity shown in the app, but also several advanced features such as:

  • AI configuration
  • prompt templates
  • guideline builder access

docs.title: What the Physician Profile contains
docs.text:
Typical information stored in the Physician Profile includes:

  • physician name
  • specialty
  • license or professional identifier
  • contact details
  • clinic-related information (if used)

This information may appear in:

  • generated reports
  • exported documentation
  • clinical summaries

docs.title: Why this section matters
docs.text:
The Physician Profile is not just an identity card.

It also acts as the configuration center for several key systems in CareFlow Kids:

  • the AI assistant
  • the prompt template
  • the guideline builder
  • API connection settings for online AI providers

docs.title: Physician identity in reports
docs.text:
When a report is generated, the physician name stored in the Physician Profile is included in the report.

This allows the report to clearly indicate which physician produced the consultation record.

At the moment, only the physician name is used for report identification. Additional professional information (such as specialty or clinic details) may be supported in future versions.

docs.title: AI assistant settings
docs.text:
The Physician Profile contains the configuration for the AI assistant.

Typical settings include:

  • API endpoint
  • model name
  • API key
  • prompt template for AI answers

These settings are only used when the physician explicitly requests AI assistance.

If AI is not configured, the rest of the application continues to function normally.

docs.title: Prompt template
docs.text:
The Physician Profile includes the prompt text area used to guide the AI assistant.

This prompt defines:

  • how the AI should interpret the structured clinical data
  • the expected response format
  • how cautious or evidence-focused the output should be

Because the physician controls the prompt, the AI assistant can be adapted to the practice style and clinical priorities of the user.

docs.title: Guideline builder access
docs.text:
The Physician Profile also provides access to the Guideline Builder.

From there, the physician can:

  • create local clinical rules
  • define priorities
  • use SNOMED concepts and structured keys
  • generate local recommendation flags

These rules run locally and do not require AI.

docs.title: Privacy and control
docs.text:
The Physician Profile centralizes configuration while keeping the physician in control.

In particular:

  • AI is optional
  • no AI request is sent automatically
  • local guideline rules can function without internet access
  • clinical responsibility always remains with the physician

docs.title: Reports
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids can generate structured clinical reports from visit data.

Reports provide a human‑readable clinical summary derived from the structured medical record stored inside the .pemr bundle.

Reports are designed to be:

  • human‑readable
  • medically structured
  • portable
  • easy to share with families

docs.title: How reports are generated
docs.text:
Reports are generated on demand from the structured clinical data stored in the patient database.

They are not stored as permanent PDF files inside the .pemr bundle.

Instead, the bundle stores the structured visit data, and the application generates the report when needed.

This design has several advantages:

  • .pemr files remain very lightweight, even for patients with many visits
  • reports can be regenerated with updated formatting
  • both CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids can produce the same report from the same data
flowchart LR
    A[Structured visit data<br>stored in db.sqlite]
    B[Report generator]
    C[Formatted clinical report]
    D[PDF export]
    E[DOCX export]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E

docs.title: Sick visit reports
docs.text:
Generated from illness episodes.

Typical contents include:

  • patient demographics
  • presenting symptoms
  • vital signs
  • physical examination findings
  • working diagnosis
  • treatments or prescriptions
  • clinical summary

docs.title: Well visit reports
docs.text:
Generated from preventive care visits.

Typical contents include:

  • growth measurements
  • growth charts
  • milestone assessment
  • feeding and sleep summary
  • physical examination findings
  • preventive guidance

docs.title: Generating a report
docs.text:
Reports are generated from the visit list.

Typical workflow:

  1. Open the patient bundle.
  2. Select the visit in the visit list.
  3. Click the Details button.
  4. Generate the report in the desired format.

Reports can be generated at any time, even long after the visit was recorded.

docs.title: Export formats
docs.text:
Reports can be exported as:

  • PDF (most common format)
  • DOCX (editable format)

These formats allow reports to be:

  • printed
  • emailed
  • stored in external systems

docs.title: Reports in CareView Kids
docs.text:
When a family opens a .pemr bundle in CareView Kids, the application can generate the same reports directly from the stored data.

Parents can therefore:

  • view visit summaries
  • export reports
  • keep medical documentation for school, insurance, or other healthcare providers

docs.title: App settings
docs.text:
The Settings section of CareFlow Kids provides tools for accessing help and troubleshooting the application.

These settings do not change patient medical data, but they allow clinicians to quickly access documentation and share technical information if support is needed.

docs.title: Accessing settings
docs.text:
Settings can be accessed from the clinician menu in the top-right corner of the application.

From this menu, clinicians can:

  • modify their profile
  • switch clinician accounts
  • access support tools
  • log out of the application

docs.title: Quick help access
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids includes a built-in help viewer that provides guidance on how to use the application.

This help system explains:

  • the typical clinical workflow
  • how patient bundles work
  • how visits and reports are created
  • how bundles are exported and shared

The help window can be opened directly from the application menu and provides a quick reference for clinicians.

docs.title: Support log
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids can generate a support log to help diagnose technical problems.

The support log may include technical information such as:

  • application activity
  • error messages
  • diagnostic information useful for troubleshooting

docs.title: Exporting the support log
docs.text:
If technical support is required, the clinician can export the support log from the menu:

  1. Open the clinician menu.
  2. Select Export support log.
  3. Share the generated file with the support team if requested.

This helps developers or technical support understand and resolve issues more quickly.

docs.title: Test error (temporary tool)
docs.text:
In some development versions of the application, a test error trigger may be available.

This tool intentionally generates a test error to verify that logging and diagnostic systems are working correctly.

This feature is mainly intended for development and debugging.

docs.title: Privacy note
docs.text:
Support logs may contain technical diagnostic information about the application session.

They should only be shared with trusted technical support contacts.

docs.title: Sick visits
docs.text:
A sick visit documents an episode of illness (an acute problem) for a child.

Examples: - fever - respiratory infection - rash - abdominal pain - injury

Each sick visit becomes part of the patient’s longitudinal record stored in the .pemr bundle.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Select the patient's bundle in the sidebar
  2. Create a new episode (choose Sick visit)
  3. Save the visit to enable vitals recording
  4. Choose the visit mode: in-person or telemedicine
  5. Enter history and symptoms
  6. Record physical examination findings
  7. Generate the problem listing (use the Problem listing button to populate the field automatically)
  8. Review guideline matching flags (if enabled — see the Guidelines section of the manual)
  9. Review AI clinical suggestions (optional support — see the AI section of the manual)

  10. Save the episode and click End / Done to close the examination form

  11. Generate a report (PDF/DOCX) when needed by clicking on the details button in the consultation list view.

  12. Reports are generated on demand from the structured data (they are not stored as static PDFs in the bundle).
  13. Export the updated .pemr bundle to share the cumulative record with the family

Good practice

After any meaningful change (new episode, edited demographics, added document), export the updated .pemr so the family always has the latest record.

docs.text:

  • Physical examination
  • AI clinical support

docs.title: AI clinical support
docs.text:
This page explains how AI assistance works during a sick visit in CareFlow Kids.

AI support is designed to assist the clinician by analyzing the structured clinical data recorded during the visit.

The system does not modify the patient record automatically and always requires clinical interpretation by the physician.

docs.title: AI pipeline (at a glance)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
  A[Structured visit data<br>chief complaint, HPI, vitals, PE, context] --> B[Episode JSON<br>AI snapshot]
  B --> C[Local guideline rules<br>JSON logic]
  C --> D[Guideline flags / indicators]
  B --> E[Physician prompt template<br>from Physician Profile]
  E --> F[Online AI provider]
  F --> G[Structured AI answer]

  D -. informs .-> G

This diagram summarizes the sequence: structured data → JSON → local guideline rules → prompt template → online AI → clinician-reviewed output.

docs.title: How the AI receives clinical information
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids collects structured clinical data during the consultation, including:

  • chief complaint
  • history of present illness
  • vital signs
  • physical examination findings
  • contextual factors (travel, sick contacts, daycare, etc.)
  • problem listing

All of this information is assembled into a structured JSON representation of the episode, generated from the structured fields recorded during the consultation.

This structured format allows the AI system to analyze the consultation in a consistent and reproducible way.

docs.title: Episode JSON representation
docs.text:
Before sending a request to the AI model, CareFlow Kids generates a JSON snapshot of the episode.

This JSON includes:

  • patient age
  • key symptoms
  • abnormal vital signs
  • abnormal physical findings
  • generated problem listing
  • contextual information

The JSON representation can be viewed using:

“AI JSON preview”.

This allows the clinician to see exactly what information is provided to the AI system.

docs.title: AI prompt configuration
docs.text:
The structure of the AI response is controlled by a clinical prompt template.

This prompt is defined in the Physician Profile section of the application.

The prompt instructs the AI to:

  • rely on trusted medical sources
  • provide structured answers
  • avoid speculation when evidence is weak

Typical response structure may include:

  1. Summary answer
  2. Evidence snapshot
  3. Actionable suggestions
  4. Reference note
  5. Caution flags

This configuration allows each physician to control how the AI assistant behaves.

docs.title: Recommendation indicators (local rules)
docs.text:
Before calling the AI model, CareFlow Kids can run local guideline rules, which can be setup by the physician using the guideline builder in the app (found in the physician profile).

These rules are executed locally using the structured clinical data.

They may generate:

  • recommendation indicators
  • clinical alerts
  • guideline flags

These rules help detect situations such as:

  • missing investigations
  • red flags
  • guideline-based recommendations

They operate independently from the AI model.

These rules are evaluated before any AI request is made, allowing guideline checks to run locally even without AI access.

docs.title: Requesting AI assistance
docs.text:
When the clinician clicks Ask AI:

  1. The episode JSON is generated.
  2. The physician prompt template is applied.
  3. The request is sent to the configured online AI provider.

Besides the clinical information contained in the episode, the AI request includes only the patient age and sex.

No direct identifiers are transmitted, such as:

  • family name
  • given name
  • alias
  • MRN

The AI then returns a structured answer based on the clinical context.

docs.title: Important clinical safety note
docs.text:
AI output is informational only.

It may depend on:

  • the quality of the entered clinical data
  • the prompt configuration
  • the medical knowledge available to the model

Clinical decisions must always remain under the responsibility of the physician.

docs.title: Relationship with the "Ask AI" section
docs.text:
This page describes AI usage within the sick visit workflow.

More detailed documentation about:

  • configuring AI providers
  • prompt design
  • advanced AI usage

is available in the Ask AI section of the manual.
