docs.title: Creating a sick visit
docs.text:
This page explains how to start and complete a sick visit episode in CareFlow Kids and describes the main sections of the episode form.

A sick visit represents a consultation for an acute medical problem (fever, cough, rash, abdominal pain, etc.).

docs.title: Starting a new episode
docs.text:
1. Select the patient bundle in the sidebar.
  2. Click New sick visit.
  3. The episode form opens.
  4. Click Save once to initialize the visit.

Saving the episode activates:

  • vitals recording
  • problem listing generation
  • guideline evaluation
  • report generation

docs.title: Sections of the sick visit form
docs.text: The episode form is structured so the consultation can follow a natural clinical workflow.

docs.title: Consultation mode
docs.text:
Select how the consultation takes place:

  • In-person (default)
  • Telemedicine

This information may appear in reports and helps contextualize the clinical examination.

docs.title: Chief complaint
docs.text:
The Motif principal section records the main reason for consultation.

You can:

  • select one or more common complaints (fever, cough, vomiting, rash, etc.)
  • add additional complaints in the free text field
  • add a duration for each complaints (hours or days)

These entries help generate the problem listing later in the workflow.

docs.title: History of present illness
docs.text:
This section captures the clinical context of the illness.

Typical structured elements include:

  • general condition
  • feeding
  • respiration
  • urination
  • pain
  • stools

Additional contextual tags may include:

  • travel
  • sick contacts
  • daycare exposure

A free text summary field allows a narrative synthesis of the history.

docs.title: Physical examination
docs.text:
The examination panel records structured findings across organ systems.

Typical sections include:

  • general appearance
  • hydration
  • hemodynamic status
  • respiratory effort
  • skin
  • ENT findings
  • lungs
  • heart
  • abdomen
  • neurologic status
  • lymph nodes

A free text complement on physical examination allows relevant details on physical finding to be added.

Many findings are selected using structured clinical tags, which allows:

  • automated problem listing
  • guideline rule matching
  • AI clinical support

docs.title: Problem listing
docs.text:
Click Generate problem listing to automatically summarize relevant clinical findings.

The system analyzes locally:

  • symptoms
  • physical examination findings
  • contextual information

The generated list can be edited manually if needed.

docs.title: Guideline signals
docs.text:
Click Update guideline flags to evaluate the episode against available clinical guidelines.

This may generate:

  • rule alerts
  • clinical indicators

These signals support clinical reasoning but do not modify the record automatically.

docs.title: Complementary investigations
docs.text:
This field records any investigations performed or planned in a free text form.

Examples:

  • blood tests
  • urine analysis
  • imaging

Results may be documented directly or attached later as documents.

docs.title: Working diagnosis
docs.text:
The working diagnosis summarizes the clinician's interpretation of the episode.

You may also record:

  • an ICD‑10 code (optional), manually or after a suggestion from AI if AI has been queried.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
The Plan field records the clinical management.

Examples:

  • treatment
  • follow‑up plan
  • parental guidance

A structured anticipatory guidance selector may also be used for common items (URI, UTI, ...), signalling which information has been communicated to the family.

docs.title: AI clinical assistant
docs.text:
The AI assistant can provide:

  • clinical suggestions
  • differential diagnosis ideas
  • guidance based on entered data

AI output is informational only and should always be reviewed with clinical judgment.

docs.title: Finishing the visit
docs.text:
1. Review the episode.
  2. Click Save.
  3. Click Done / End to close the form.

The episode is now part of the patient's longitudinal medical record.

You may then:

  • generate a report from the visit list (Details button)
  • export the updated .pemr bundle to share the cumulative record with the family

docs.title: Deleting or restoring a visit
docs.text:
Episodes can be deleted or restored from the visit list.

To do this:

  1. Open the visit list for the patient.
  2. Right‑click the visit you want to manage.
  3. Choose Delete or Restore depending on the situation.

Deleting a visit does not necessarily mean the information is permanently lost. A deleted visit can be restored later if needed.

This allows the clinician to correct mistakes (for example a visit created by accident) without permanently removing the clinical information.

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Episode summary (visit details)
docs.text:
This page explains the Episode summary screen (also called Visit details) for a sick visit in CareFlow Kids.

The episode summary is the clinician-facing, read-only overview of the visit, and it is also the entry point for exporting a report for that specific episode.

docs.title: How to open the episode summary
docs.text:
1. Open the patient bundle.
  2. Go to the visit list.
  3. Click the Details button for the visit you want to review.

This opens Visit details for the selected episode.

docs.title: What the episode summary shows
docs.text:
The summary view displays key information such as:

  • episode ID
  • visit date
  • visit category (sick visit)
  • the generated problem / summary block

The Problems / Summary block is a compact clinical synthesis built from the structured data recorded during the visit.

It typically includes:

  • patient age and sex
  • main complaint(s)
  • duration (if recorded)
  • key history and context items
  • relevant examination findings

This gives a fast overview of the episode without reopening the full episode form.

docs.title: Exporting a report for the episode
docs.text:
From the episode summary screen, you can export a report for this specific visit.

  1. Click Export.
  2. Choose the desired format:
  3. PDF (print-friendly)
  4. DOCX (editable)
  5. Save or share the generated report.

Reports are generated on demand from the structured data stored in the database.

They are not stored as static files inside the .pemr bundle.

docs.title: Typical use cases
docs.text:
Export a visit report when you need:

  • a shareable summary for parents
  • a document for school or childcare
  • a clean clinical note for your own archive

docs.title: Screenshots
docs.text: Screenshot (TODO)

docs.title: Physical examination
docs.text:
This page explains how to record the physical examination findings during a sick visit in CareFlow Kids.

The examination section allows clinicians to document structured clinical findings across multiple organ systems.

docs.title: Physical examination mode
docs.text:
At the beginning of the examination section, the clinician must select how the physical examination (PE) was performed.

Available options are:

  • In‑person examination
  • Remote examination
  • Not assessed

The selected mode indicates how the clinical findings were obtained and may appear in reports.

docs.title: Telemedicine visits
docs.text:
When the visit is created in telemedicine mode, the PE mode is restricted to:

  • Remote examination
  • Not assessed

This reflects the fact that a full in‑person examination cannot be performed during a remote consultation.

docs.title: Structured examination approach
docs.text:
The examination form is organized by clinical systems, allowing the consultation to follow a natural workflow.

Typical sections include:

  • general appearance
  • hydration
  • hemodynamic status
  • respiratory effort
  • skin
  • ENT (ear, nose, throat)
  • lungs
  • heart
  • abdomen
  • neurologic status
  • lymph nodes

Each system contains predefined clinical findings that can be selected quickly.

docs.title: Selecting examination findings
docs.text:
Most examination findings are recorded by clicking predefined clinical tags.

Examples include:

  • "Normal"
  • "Crackles"
  • "Wheezing"
  • "Rash"
  • "Abdominal tenderness"

Selecting structured findings ensures that clinical data remains consistent and machine-readable.

docs.title: Multiple findings in the same system
docs.text:
Several findings can be selected within the same examination section.

For example, in the lung examination:

  • crackles
  • wheezing
  • decreased breath sounds

This allows precise documentation of complex clinical presentations.

docs.title: Using "Normal"
docs.text:
Most systems include a Normal, often the default option.

Selecting "Normal" indicates that no abnormal findings were detected in that system.

If abnormal findings are selected, the "Normal" option should usually not be selected.

docs.title: Free text examination notes
docs.text:
Additional examination details can be recorded in the free text clinical notes field.

This allows clinicians to:

  • clarify unusual findings
  • describe nuances not covered by structured tags
  • summarize the examination when necessary

Structured findings and free text can be used together.

docs.title: How examination findings are used
docs.text: Recorded findings contribute to several automated processes in CareFlow Kids:

docs.title: Problem listing
docs.text: Selected symptoms and examination findings can be summarized automatically in the problem listing.

docs.title: Guideline evaluation
docs.text: Clinical rules can analyze findings and generate guideline flags.

docs.title: AI clinical support
docs.text: The AI assistant may use examination findings to provide clinical suggestions or differential diagnoses.

docs.title: Editing examination findings
docs.text:
Examination findings can be modified at any time and problem listing update while the episode form remains open and when reopening an episode.

Simply:

  1. Select or deselect the appropriate findings.
  2. Click Save to update the episode.

docs.title: Addendum
docs.text:
An Addendum can be used to record additional information about the episode after the initial consultation has been completed.

This is useful when:

  • follow‑up information becomes available
  • laboratory or imaging results are received later
  • the clinician needs to clarify or complete the documentation

To add an addendum:

  1. Reopen the episode.
  2. Navigate to the Addendum section at the bottom of the form.
  3. Enter the additional information.
  4. Save the episode.

The addendum becomes part of the episode record while preserving the original clinical documentation.

docs.title: Recording vitals
docs.text:
This page explains how to record vital signs during a sick visit in CareFlow Kids.

Vital signs provide objective clinical information about the child's condition and are automatically evaluated against age‑appropriate reference ranges.

docs.title: When vitals can be recorded
docs.text:
Vitals can only be recorded after the episode has been saved once.

When the episode is first created, the vitals section shows a message indicating that the visit must be saved before vitals can be entered.

Saving the episode initializes the data structure needed to store vital measurements.

docs.title: Typical vital signs recorded
docs.text:
Common measurements include:

  • Weight
  • Height / Length
  • Head circumference
  • Temperature
  • Heart rate (HR)
  • Respiratory rate (RR)
  • SpO₂
  • Blood pressure (optional)

These values are stored in the patient record and may be used in:

  • growth charts
  • clinical evaluation
  • report generation

docs.title: Automatic abnormal value detection
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids automatically evaluates vital signs against age‑specific reference ranges.

If a value falls outside the expected range:

  • the value is flagged as abnormal
  • a corresponding clinical signal may be generated
  • the abnormality may appear in the problem listing

This helps clinicians quickly identify important physiological abnormalities.

docs.title: Multiple measurements during the same visit
docs.text:
Vital signs may be recorded multiple times during a single episode.

This is useful when:

  • monitoring a child during observation
  • repeating a measurement to confirm accuracy
  • recording vitals after treatment (for example after antipyretics)

Each measurement is stored with a timestamp in the vitals history.

docs.title: Replacing previous measurements
docs.text:
When recording new vitals, the clinician can choose whether to:

  • add a new measurement, or
  • replace the previous measurement for this episode

Replacing a measurement updates the value used for the current episode while preserving the clinical workflow.

docs.title: Vitals history
docs.text:
The vitals panel displays a history of measurements for the episode.

Entries are displayed from oldest to most recent, allowing the clinician to quickly review changes over time.

docs.title: Accessing vitals from the main dashboard
docs.text:
All vitals recorded for the patient can also be accessed from the main dashboard.

To view the full vitals history:

  1. Open the Measures section in the dashboard.
  2. Click Vitals.

This view displays the complete history of vitals recorded for the patient, across all visits.

This allows the clinician to quickly review:

  • past measurements
  • physiological trends
  • previously recorded abnormal values

docs.title: Saving vitals
docs.text:
After entering measurements:

  1. Click Save vitals.

The values will then appear in the episode record and may influence:

  • problem listing
  • guideline signals
  • AI clinical suggestions

docs.title: Well visits
docs.text:
Well visits document preventive pediatric care and follow the child's growth, development, and general health over time.

In CareFlow Kids, well visits are designed to support the structured follow-up of children at expected age milestones.

docs.title: Main components
docs.text:
Well visits typically include:

  • Growth monitoring
  • Developmental milestones
  • Feeding and nutrition
  • Sleep
  • Physical examination
  • Preventive guidance

These elements are combined into a structured record that can be reviewed over time and included in reports.

docs.title: Typical workflow
docs.text:
1. Open the patient bundle in the sidebar.
  2. Enter growth measurements in the Growth Table from the dashboard Measures section.

Growth measurements should be recorded in the Growth Table before opening a new well visit, so that the visit can immediately display the growth trend analysis.

  1. Create or open the relevant well visit.
  2. Record feeding, sleep, and physical examination findings.
  3. Review developmental milestones.
  4. Write conclusions and guidance.
  5. Save the visit.
  6. Generate a report if needed.
  7. Export the updated .pemr bundle to keep the portable record current.

Good practice

Record growth measurements as close as possible to the expected visit date so that the growth evaluation and milestone interpretation remain clinically accurate.

docs.title: Relationship with the patient record
docs.text:
Each well visit becomes part of the patient's longitudinal preventive health record stored in the .pemr bundle.

Over time, this allows the clinician to review:

  • growth trajectories
  • developmental progress
  • previous advice and conclusions
  • continuity of preventive care

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Creating a well visit
  • Growth monitoring
  • Milestone assessment
  • Visit summary

docs.title: Creating a well visit
docs.text:
This page explains how to start and complete a well visit in CareFlow Kids.

A well visit is a preventive pediatric consultation focused on growth, development, nutrition, physical examination, and anticipatory guidance.

Unlike a sick visit, the goal is not to evaluate an acute illness, but to assess the child's overall health and developmental progress at an expected age milestone.

docs.title: Before opening the visit
docs.text:
1. Select the patient bundle in the sidebar.
  2. Open the Growth Table from the dashboard Measures section.
  3. Enter the most recent growth measurements (weight, height/length, head circumference when available).

Recording growth measurements first is important because the well visit will immediately display:

  • growth chart positioning
  • growth trend analysis
  • weight gain interpretation when relevant

Entering the measurements before opening the visit ensures that the growth evaluation shown during the visit is accurate.

docs.title: Starting a well visit
docs.text:
1. Select the patient bundle in the sidebar (if not already selected).
  2. Create or open the relevant well visit.
  3. Choose the age‑specific visit type (for example: newborn visit, 1‑month visit, 2‑month visit, etc.).
  4. The structured well visit form opens.
  5. Review the growth information automatically retrieved from the Growth Table.

Well visits are organized around expected pediatric follow‑up ages. Each visit type focuses on the clinical questions most relevant for that stage of development.

For example:

  • early visits focus on feeding, weight gain, and early adaptation
  • infant visits emphasize motor and social developmental milestones
  • later visits focus more on long‑term growth and developmental progression

This age‑structured design helps guide the clinician through the preventive evaluation appropriate for each stage.

docs.title: Main sections of the well visit form
docs.text: The well visit form is organized around preventive pediatric follow-up.

docs.title: Growth
docs.text:
The visit displays the patient's available growth data and associated growth analysis.

This may include:

  • weight trend
  • height / length trend
  • head circumference trend
  • z-scores and percentiles
  • weight gain analysis (especially in young infants)

docs.title: Developmental milestones
docs.text:
The clinician reviews milestone items appropriate for the patient's age.

This allows the visit to document:

  • milestones achieved
  • milestones of concern
  • areas requiring follow-up

docs.title: Feeding and nutrition
docs.text:
The visit records nutritional information such as:

  • breast or formula feeding
  • solids / complementary feeding
  • appetite or feeding issues

docs.title: Sleep
docs.text:
The visit records relevant information about:

  • sleep duration
  • sleep quality
  • sleep-related concerns

docs.title: Physical examination
docs.text: The physical examination records structured preventive findings.

docs.title: Conclusions and guidance
docs.text:
The clinician can summarize:

  • the overall preventive assessment
  • recommendations
  • anticipatory guidance for the family

docs.title: AI support (optional)
docs.text:
Well visits can also use the AI assistant when configured in the Physician Profile.

The AI receives the structured information recorded during the visit, including:

  • growth data
  • developmental milestones
  • feeding information
  • physical examination findings

A dedicated well‑visit prompt can be configured in the Physician Profile to guide how the AI analyzes this preventive data.

As with sick visits, AI responses are informational support only and clinical decisions remain under the responsibility of the physician.

docs.title: Problem listing and visit summary
docs.text:
At the end of the visit, CareFlow Kids can generate a problem listing based on the structured information recorded during the consultation.

The problem listing may include:

  • parental concerns
  • abnormal growth findings
  • developmental concerns
  • sleep or feeding issues
  • screening results (for example M‑CHAT)

This generated list helps summarize the most clinically relevant points of the visit.

After the visit is saved, the resulting summary can be reviewed from the visit list:

  1. Open the patient's visit list.
  2. Click the Details button next to the visit.

This opens the visit summary, where the problem listing and structured visit information are displayed.

docs.title: Addendum (post‑visit notes)
docs.text:
Well visits also include an Addendum section.

The addendum allows the clinician to add additional information after the visit has been completed, for example:

  • follow‑up information received later
  • clarification after reviewing test results
  • additional parental observations

Addendum entries remain linked to the original visit and become part of the longitudinal medical record.

This allows clinicians to update the visit documentation without modifying the original consultation data.

docs.title: Well visit lifecycle
docs.text:
The typical lifecycle of a well visit in CareFlow Kids can be summarized as follows:

flowchart LR
    A[Select patient bundle] --> B[Enter growth data in Growth Table]
    B --> C[Create well visit]
    C --> D[Complete structured visit form]
    D --> E[Generate problem listing]
    E --> F[Save visit]
    F --> G[Review visit summary]
    G --> H[Generate report if needed]
    H --> I[Export updated .pemr bundle]

This diagram reflects the normal preventive consultation workflow:

  • growth data entered first
  • visit completed using structured sections
  • problem listing generated automatically
  • visit saved and reviewed in the visit summary
  • report generated when required
  • updated .pemr bundle exported to keep the portable record current

docs.title: Saving the well visit
docs.text:
After completing the form:

  1. Click Save.
  2. Review the generated content if needed.
  3. Generate a report when required.

The visit then becomes part of the patient's longitudinal preventive record.

docs.title: Reports and export
docs.text:
A well visit report can be generated on demand from the visit data.

After the visit is complete, clinicians should export the updated .pemr bundle so that the portable patient record remains current.

docs.title: Growth monitoring
docs.text:
Growth monitoring is a central component of pediatric well visits in CareFlow Kids.

The application analyzes the patient's recorded measurements and compares them with WHO growth reference standards to evaluate whether growth follows an expected trajectory.

docs.title: Source of growth data
docs.text:
Growth analysis uses the measurements stored in the Growth Table.

These measurements can originate from:

  • manual entries in the Growth Table
  • measurements recorded during sick visits (vitals)
  • measurements collected during well visits

All measurements are consolidated in the Growth Table to maintain a single chronological growth history for the patient.

docs.title: Measurements used for analysis
docs.text:
Growth monitoring uses the following parameters when available:

  • Weight
  • Height / Length
  • Head circumference

Depending on the patient's age, the system may also evaluate:

  • Weight-for-length
  • BMI

These measurements are compared against WHO pediatric reference curves.

docs.title: Growth charts and percentiles
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids calculates:

  • percentile position
  • z-scores

These indicators allow clinicians to evaluate whether the patient's growth remains within expected limits.

The charts visualize:

  • growth trajectory over time
  • comparison with WHO reference curves

docs.title: Short-term growth evaluation
docs.text:
In early infancy, growth monitoring focuses particularly on short-term weight gain.

The system may evaluate:

  • recent weight gain
  • weight gain velocity

This helps identify situations such as:

  • insufficient weight gain
  • feeding difficulties
  • early nutritional concerns

docs.title: Long-term growth evaluation
docs.text:
As the child grows older, the evaluation increasingly focuses on long-term growth trajectory.

Clinicians review:

  • weight trajectory
  • height trajectory
  • proportional growth patterns

This helps detect:

  • growth faltering
  • disproportionate growth
  • possible endocrine or nutritional problems

docs.title: Integration with the well visit
docs.text:
When a well visit is opened, CareFlow Kids retrieves the available growth data from the Growth Table.

The visit can then display:

  • growth chart position
  • recent growth evolution
  • trend interpretation

For this reason, clinicians are encouraged to enter the most recent measurements before opening the visit.

docs.title: Clinical interpretation
docs.text:
Growth indicators and charts are intended to support clinical judgment.

They should always be interpreted in the broader clinical context, including:

  • feeding history
  • developmental progress
  • family growth patterns

docs.title: Milestone assessment
docs.text:
Developmental milestone assessment is a core component of pediatric well visits in CareFlow Kids.

The milestone section helps clinicians document the child's developmental progress across key domains and identify possible areas requiring closer follow‑up.

docs.title: Age‑specific milestone sets
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids organizes milestones according to the age of the well visit.

Each visit type (for example: newborn visit, 1‑month visit, 2‑month visit, etc.) presents milestone items that are relevant for that stage of development.

This age‑structured approach helps clinicians focus on the developmental abilities that are expected or emerging at that age.

Examples include:

  • early visits: basic reflexes, alertness, early interaction
  • infancy: motor development, social smiling, vocalization
  • later infancy: sitting, crawling, interaction, language emergence
  • toddler years: walking, speech development, social behaviors

Optional notes may also be recorded to document observations or parental concerns.

docs.title: Recording milestones
docs.text:
For each milestone item, the clinician can indicate whether the behavior is:

  • Achieved
  • Not yet observed
  • Uncertain / requires follow‑up (when applicable)

docs.title: Neurodevelopmental screening (M-CHAT)
docs.text:
At the relevant ages, the well visit can also record a neurodevelopmental screening result such as M-CHAT.

This allows the clinician to document:

  • the M-CHAT score
  • the corresponding risk category
  • any additional developmental screening score when used

This screening information becomes part of the structured preventive record and can support:

  • developmental surveillance
  • follow-up planning
  • referral decisions when needed

docs.title: Purpose of milestone tracking
docs.text:
Documenting milestones allows the clinician to:

  • follow developmental progress over time
  • identify potential developmental delays
  • document parental observations
  • support early referral when needed

Because each well visit becomes part of the patient's longitudinal record, milestone documentation allows clinicians to review developmental progression across multiple visits.

docs.title: Integration with the well visit summary
docs.text:
The milestone assessment contributes to the preventive evaluation recorded in the well visit.

Items that are not achieved or that raise concern may be highlighted in the clinician's conclusions and guidance section.

This allows the visit report to clearly document:

  • developmental strengths
  • areas requiring monitoring
  • recommendations for follow‑up

docs.title: Clinical interpretation
docs.text:
Milestone assessment supports clinical evaluation but should always be interpreted in context.

Relevant considerations include:

  • variation in normal developmental timing
  • prematurity when applicable
  • family observations
  • environmental context

The milestone section therefore serves as a structured support for developmental surveillance, not as a standalone diagnostic tool.

docs.title: Well visit summary
docs.text:
The well visit summary presents a structured overview of the preventive consultation.

It gathers the key information recorded during the visit and prepares the data used to generate the visit report.

The summary helps the clinician quickly review:

  • growth evaluation
  • developmental milestones
  • screening results
  • preventive guidance
  • follow‑up recommendations

docs.title: Where to access the visit summary
docs.text:
The well visit summary can be accessed from the visit list.

  1. Open the patient's visit list.
  2. Locate the relevant well visit.
  3. Click the Details button.

This opens the visit summary view, where the clinician can review the structured information recorded during the visit.

From this screen it is also possible to:

  • generate a PDF or DOCX report of the visit
  • export or share the report when needed

docs.title: What the summary includes
docs.text:
The well visit summary typically aggregates information from multiple sections of the visit:

Patient context

  • age at visit
  • visit type (1 month, 2 months, 4 months, etc.)

Growth evaluation

  • latest measurements from the Growth Table
  • growth chart positioning
  • growth trend interpretation

Developmental milestones

  • milestone items recorded during the visit
  • flagged items that may require monitoring

Neurodevelopmental screening

When applicable, screening results such as M‑CHAT may be recorded and summarized.

These results help support early identification of developmental concerns.

Clinical observations

  • feeding and nutrition
  • sleep patterns
  • parental concerns
  • general examination findings

Preventive guidance

  • anticipatory guidance
  • safety advice
  • follow‑up planning

docs.title: Editing the visit summary
docs.text:
The summary is generated from the structured information recorded during the visit.

If adjustments are needed, the clinician should update the relevant sections of the visit form rather than editing the summary directly.

This ensures that:

  • the clinical data remains consistent
  • reports generated later reflect the corrected information

docs.title: Generating the visit report
docs.text:
From the visit summary, the clinician can generate a well visit report.

Reports are produced on demand from the structured visit data and can be exported as:

  • PDF
  • DOCX

Because reports are generated dynamically, they are not stored as static files inside the .pemr bundle.

This keeps patient bundles lightweight even when many visits are recorded.

docs.title: Exporting the updated record
docs.text:
Once the well visit is complete, the clinician may export the updated .pemr bundle.

This allows the family to open the updated medical record in CareView Kids.

The bundle contains:

  • structured visit data
  • growth history
  • milestone records
  • attached documents

Families can later return an updated .pemr bundle if new information or documents are added.

docs.title: CareView Kids (Patient App)
docs.text: How parents open a bundle, view records, growth, and documents.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Open a .pemr bundle
  • View visit reports
  • View growth charts
  • View documents
  • Add a note
  • Export / share a report (PDF)
  • App settings

docs.title: Add a note
docs.text: Notes let families record information between visits (symptoms, questions, observations) and keep it with the patient’s .pemr record.

docs.title: When to add a note
docs.text:
Good examples:

  • “Fever started at 18:00, peaked at 39.2°C, responded to paracetamol.”
  • “Cough worse at night; appetite decreased.”
  • “Question for doctor: can we restart daycare tomorrow?”
  • “Rash appeared after new detergent.”

docs.title: How to add a note
docs.text:
1. Open the patient .pemr in CareView Kids
  2. Go to Notes
  3. Tap Add note
  4. Write the note (short is fine)
  5. Save

docs.title: Where the note is stored
docs.text:
• Notes are saved inside the .pemr bundle (as part of the patient record).
  • That means the note can be shared back to the clinic by exporting an updated .pemr.

docs.title: Share notes back to the doctor (optional)
docs.text:
If your clinic asks for updates:

  1. Export an updated .pemr from CareView Kids
  2. Send it to the clinic
  3. The doctor can import it into CareFlow Kids

docs.title: Tips (make notes more useful)
docs.text:
• Add a date/time if relevant (the note itself is timestamped)
  • Include measurements (temperature, weight, SpO₂ if measured)
  • Mention what helped (medication dose, hydration, rest)

docs.title: Export or share a visit report
docs.text:
CareView Kids allows parents or patients to generate and share visit reports from the medical record contained in a .pemr bundle.

Reports provide a human‑readable summary of a medical visit and can be shared with schools, caregivers, or other healthcare professionals when needed.

docs.title: Where reports come from
docs.text:
Reports are generated on demand from the structured medical data stored in the .pemr bundle.

This means that:

  • reports are not stored as permanent PDF files inside the bundle
  • the application generates the report whenever it is requested

This keeps .pemr bundles lightweight even when many visits are recorded.

docs.title: How to export a report
docs.text:
1. Open the .pemr bundle in CareView Kids.
  2. Go to the Visits section.
  3. Select the visit you want to review.
  4. Open the visit details.
  5. Tap Export report.

The application generates a PDF report that can be:

  • saved to the device
  • shared with other applications
  • printed if needed

docs.title: Typical uses
docs.text:
Parents may export a visit report to:

  • share medical information with another doctor
  • provide documentation for school or childcare
  • keep a personal copy of a consultation summary

Because reports are generated directly from the medical record, they reflect the exact information recorded during the visit.

docs.title: Relationship with the .pemr bundle
docs.text:
Exporting a report does not modify the .pemr bundle.

The bundle remains the complete portable medical record.

Reports are simply temporary documents generated from the stored data.

docs.title: Privacy reminder
docs.text:
Medical reports contain personal health information.

Before sharing a report, make sure it is sent only to trusted recipients.

docs.title: Open a .pemr bundle
docs.text:
CareView Kids allows parents or patients to open a .pemr bundle, which contains the child's portable medical record.

A .pemr file typically comes from the doctor after a consultation using CareFlow Kids.

Opening the bundle in CareView Kids allows the family to:

  • review visit summaries
  • view growth charts
  • access attached documents
  • add personal notes

docs.title: What a .pemr bundle contains
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle is a portable patient record that may include:

  • structured visit data
  • growth measurements and charts
  • developmental milestone records
  • attached medical documents

The bundle is designed so the complete record can move safely between:

  • CareFlow Kids (doctor app)
  • CareView Kids (patient viewer)

docs.title: How to open a bundle
docs.text: CareView Kids allows two ways to access a patient record.

docs.title: 1. Import a new bundle
docs.text:
When a doctor sends an updated .pemr file, it should first be imported into the app.

  1. Launch CareView Kids.
  2. Tap Load a new bundle from the device.
  3. Select the .pemr file from Files or iCloud.

The application then imports the bundle into its local library.

If a bundle for the same patient already exists, the app may ask whether you want to replace the existing bundle.

In most cases, you should confirm the replacement if the new bundle is more recent.

Once imported, the bundle opens automatically and the different sections of the medical record become available.

docs.title: 2. Open a bundle from the library
docs.text:
After a bundle has been imported once, it is stored in the local bundle library of the device.

To reopen a previously imported record:

  1. Tap Load from saved bundles.
  2. Select the patient record from the list.

The bundle will open and you can review visits, growth charts, documents, and notes.

The library can contain bundles for multiple children, allowing families with several children to keep their records together on the same device.

docs.title: Where bundles usually come from
docs.text:
Most families receive .pemr bundles from their clinic after a consultation.

The doctor exports the updated record using CareFlow Kids and shares it with the family.

The bundle can then be:

  • stored on a phone or computer
  • reopened later in CareView Kids
  • shared again with the clinic when needed by the doctor

docs.title: Keeping the bundle safe
docs.text:
The .pemr file is the complete portable medical record for the child.

It is recommended to keep a backup copy of the bundle in a safe location such as:

  • a computer
  • a phone or tablet
  • a secure cloud storage location

docs.title: Important note
docs.text:
Although .pemr files are technically archive files, they should normally not be opened or modified manually outside the application.

The safest way to access the medical record is through CareView Kids.

docs.title: App settings
docs.text:
The Settings section of CareView Kids allows families to configure privacy, support, and help options for the app.

These settings do not change the medical content of the .pemr bundle itself, but they help protect access to the app and make troubleshooting easier when needed.

docs.title: Accessing settings
docs.text:
Settings can be opened from the main CareView Kids screen.

Typical uses include:

  • enabling app protection
  • managing support logs
  • opening the help section

docs.title: App protection
docs.text:
CareView Kids can protect access to the app using a passcode and, when supported by the device, Face ID / biometric unlock.

This helps prevent unauthorized access to the child's medical record.

docs.title: Setting a passcode
docs.text:
To enable app lock:

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Go to App lock.
  3. Enter a new passcode.
  4. Confirm the passcode.

Once enabled, the app will require the passcode (or biometric unlock when available) before access is granted.

docs.title: Why app lock matters
docs.text:
The .pemr bundle contains sensitive personal health information.

Using a passcode or Face ID helps protect:

  • visit reports
  • growth charts
  • attached medical documents
  • parent notes

docs.title: Support logging
docs.text:
CareView Kids can record a support log to help diagnose technical problems.

The support log is intended for troubleshooting and may help identify:

  • import problems
  • display issues
  • unexpected app behavior

docs.title: Available actions
docs.text:
From the Settings screen, families can:

  • Enable or disable support logging
  • Share the support log
  • Clear the support log

docs.title: When to use support logging
docs.text:
Support logging should usually be enabled only when:

  • the app is not working correctly
  • the clinic or developer asks for diagnostic information

After the issue is resolved, logging can be turned off again.

docs.title: Help access
docs.text:
The Settings screen also provides quick access to the Help section.

This makes it easy to find guidance about:

  • opening a .pemr bundle
  • viewing visits, growth charts, or documents
  • exporting reports
  • using notes or other app features

docs.title: Privacy reminder
docs.text:
Because the app may contain sensitive medical information, families should:

  • keep the device protected
  • enable app lock when possible
  • share support logs only with trusted support contacts

docs.title: View documents
docs.text:
CareView Kids allows parents to access medical documents attached to the child's record.

These documents are part of the .pemr bundle and may include files uploaded by the doctor or added by the family.

Typical documents include:

  • vaccination records
  • laboratory reports
  • imaging reports
  • hospital discharge summaries
  • other medical documents relevant to the child's care

docs.title: Accessing documents
docs.text:
After opening a .pemr bundle:

  1. Open the patient record in CareView Kids.
  2. Tap View documents.

The application displays the list of documents stored in the patient's record.

docs.title: Opening a document
docs.text:
To read a document:

  1. Tap the document in the list.
  2. The file opens using the appropriate viewer on the device.

Depending on the file type, the document may open as:

  • PDF
  • image
  • text document

This allows parents to easily review medical information provided during consultations.

docs.title: Documents added by the doctor
docs.text:
Doctors may attach documents to the patient record using CareFlow Kids.

These documents are then included in the exported .pemr bundle and become visible in CareView Kids.

docs.title: Documents added by parents
docs.text:
CareView Kids also allow parents to add documents to the bundle.

For example, families may add:

  • vaccination certificates
  • laboratory results obtained elsewhere
  • specialist reports

When the updated .pemr bundle is later shared with the doctor, these documents become available in CareFlow Kids.

docs.title: Document privacy
docs.text:
Documents stored in the .pemr bundle may contain sensitive medical information.

Parents should ensure the bundle is stored in a safe location and shared only with trusted healthcare professionals.

docs.title: View growth charts
docs.text:
CareView Kids allows parents to follow the child's growth over time using the measurements recorded by the doctor.

Growth data is part of the .pemr medical record and comes from measurements entered during medical visits or added to the growth table by the clinician.

This section helps families understand how the child is growing compared with normal pediatric growth patterns.

docs.title: Accessing growth charts
docs.text:
After opening a .pemr bundle:

  1. Open the patient record in CareView Kids.
  2. Tap View growth charts.

The application displays charts that show how the child's measurements evolve over time.

docs.title: What measurements are shown
docs.text:
Growth charts are built from measurements recorded in the medical record, including:

  • Weight
  • Height / Length
  • Head circumference
  • Body mass index

Each point on the chart corresponds to a measurement taken during a consultation or entered in the growth table.

docs.title: Understanding the charts
docs.text:
The charts compare the child's measurements to standard pediatric growth references.

This helps visualize:

  • whether growth follows a normal pattern
  • whether growth is accelerating or slowing
  • how measurements compare with children of the same age

Growth charts are primarily used by doctors to interpret development, but they also help families visualize long‑term growth trends.

docs.title: Why growth tracking matters
docs.text:
Tracking growth helps monitor important aspects of a child's health, including:

  • nutritional status
  • normal physical development
  • early detection of growth concerns

Because the data is stored in the .pemr bundle, growth history remains available even if the record is shared between devices.

docs.title: View visit reports
docs.text:
CareView Kids allows parents to review the medical visits recorded for the child.

These visits come from the .pemr bundle provided by the doctor and represent the consultations performed in CareFlow Kids.

The visit section allows families to:

  • review the report of each medical visit
  • understand the doctor's observations
  • keep a readable record of consultations

docs.title: Accessing the visit list
docs.text:
After opening a .pemr bundle, visits can be accessed from the main dashboard.

  1. Open the patient record in CareView Kids.
  2. Tap View visits.

The application displays the visit list, which contains all recorded consultations for that child.

docs.title: Understanding the visit list
docs.text:
The visit list displays visits in chronological order.

Each item shows:

  • the date of the consultation
  • the type of visit (for example well visit or sick visit)
  • a short label describing the visit

Two categories of visits may appear:

  • Well visits – preventive consultations focused on growth and development
  • Sick visits – consultations related to illness

This separation helps families quickly identify the type of consultation.

docs.title: Opening a visit report
docs.text:
To review the details of a consultation:

  1. Tap the visit in the list.
  2. The visit report opens.

The visit report presents the key elements recorded by the doctor during the consultation.

The report may include:

  • main concerns discussed during the visit
  • growth evaluation
  • developmental observations
  • examination findings
  • medical guidance or recommendations

docs.title: Visit reports
docs.text:
Visit reports are generated on demand from the structured clinical data recorded during the consultation.

From the visit report screen, it is possible to:

  • read the visit report
  • export the PDF version of the report if needed

Reports provide a clean, structured version of the consultation that can be shared or archived.

docs.title: Why visits are important
docs.text:
Keeping the visit history allows families to:

  • follow the child's health over time
  • review advice provided during previous consultations
  • share medical information with other healthcare professionals when necessary

Because the visit history is stored inside the .pemr bundle, it remains portable and private.

docs.title: Privacy & Security
docs.text: How data is stored, shared, and protected.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Local-first model
  • Sharing bundles safely
  • Encryption (if enabled)
  • What the AI can/can’t do
  • Localization
  • Intended Use & Scope of the Software

docs.title: AI safety and limitations
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids includes optional AI clinical assistance to help clinicians review structured medical information.

AI support is designed as a decision‑support tool, not an automated medical decision system.

The clinician always remains responsible for the final medical interpretation and clinical decisions.

docs.title: When AI is used
docs.text:
The AI system is only used when the clinician explicitly requests it, for example by clicking Ask AI during a consultation.

AI is not automatically running in the background and does not continuously analyze patient data.

This means that clinical information is only shared with the AI provider when the clinician chooses to request assistance.

docs.title: What information the AI receives
docs.text:
When AI assistance is requested, CareFlow Kids generates a structured JSON representation of the visit.

This structured information may include:

  • patient age
  • patient sex
  • symptoms and complaints
  • vital signs
  • physical examination findings
  • growth information
  • relevant history (for example perinatal history or past medical history)
  • problem listing generated during the visit

This structured format helps the AI analyze the clinical context more reliably.

docs.title: What information is NOT sent
docs.text:
Direct personal identifiers are not included in the AI request.

The following information is not transmitted to the AI provider:

  • patient name
  • family name
  • alias
  • MRN (medical record number)

This reduces the exposure of possible identifiable patient information.

docs.title: Local guideline checks
docs.text:
Before contacting an online AI model, CareFlow Kids can run local guideline rules.

These rules operate entirely on the clinician's device and may:

  • detect red flags
  • suggest guideline-based actions
  • highlight potential diagnoses

Because these rules run locally, no patient information is transmitted externally when they are executed.

docs.title: AI limitations
docs.text:
AI systems have important limitations.

AI responses may depend on:

  • the completeness of the clinical information entered
  • the configuration of the physician prompt
  • the knowledge available to the AI model

AI suggestions may therefore be:

  • incomplete
  • overly general
  • occasionally incorrect

For this reason, AI output should always be interpreted as supporting information, not a definitive diagnosis.

docs.title: Clinical responsibility
docs.text:
Medical decisions must always remain under the responsibility of the clinician.

The AI system cannot:

  • make medical diagnoses
  • prescribe treatments
  • replace clinical judgment

Its role is limited to helping the clinician review and organize clinical information.

docs.title: Privacy considerations
docs.text:
When AI is used, structured clinical information is transmitted to the configured AI provider.

Clinicians should therefore:

  • review the information before requesting AI assistance
  • use AI only when clinically appropriate
  • follow applicable medical privacy regulations

docs.title: Encryption and integrity protection
docs.text:
The .pemr bundle is designed to protect sensitive medical information.

A key part of this protection is the security of the structured medical database stored inside the bundle.

docs.title: What is protected
docs.text:
Inside a .pemr bundle, the main structured medical record is stored in:

  • db.sqlite

This database contains information such as:

  • visits
  • growth data
  • notes
  • other structured clinical information

Because this data is sensitive, it is protected by encryption and integrity verification.

docs.title: Database encryption
docs.text:
The db.sqlite file is encrypted.

Encryption helps protect the clinical data when the bundle is:

  • stored on a device
  • transferred between doctor and family
  • kept as a backup copy

This reduces the risk of the structured medical information being read directly outside the application.

docs.title: Integrity protection
docs.text:
In addition to encryption, the bundle uses HMAC / SHA-256 based integrity protection.

This helps verify that the database has not been:

  • altered unexpectedly
  • corrupted during transfer
  • modified outside the application

If integrity verification fails, the application may refuse to open the bundle or may report an error.

docs.title: Why integrity matters
docs.text:
Medical data must remain both:

  • confidential
  • trustworthy

Encryption protects confidentiality.

Integrity protection helps ensure that the record received by the doctor or family is the same record that was originally saved.

This is especially important when bundles are shared between devices.

docs.title: Relationship with manual editing
docs.text:
Although a .pemr bundle is technically a file container, it should not be modified manually.

Opening or editing internal files outside CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids may:

  • break integrity verification
  • make the bundle unreadable
  • damage the medical record

Bundles should only be changed through the application interface.

docs.title: Practical meaning for users
docs.text:
For clinicians and families, this means:

  • the structured patient record is protected
  • the application checks that the record has not been tampered with
  • manually changing bundle contents is unsafe

For everyday use, the safest practice is simply to:

  • keep the .pemr file in a secure place
  • share it only with trusted recipients
  • use the application to read or update it

docs.title: Intended Use & Scope of the Software
docs.text:
This document describes the intended use and scope of CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids.

This section clarifies how the software is designed to be used and the limits of its intended functionality.

docs.title: Intended use
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids are designed as clinical documentation and medical record organization tools.

Their purpose is to help clinicians and families:

  • record structured medical information
  • generate readable visit reports
  • monitor growth and development
  • organize medical documents
  • maintain portable patient records using .pemr bundles

The software is intended to assist the documentation and organization of clinical information, not to replace clinical judgment.

docs.title: Target users
docs.text:
The applications are intended for:

CareFlow Kids

  • licensed physicians
  • healthcare professionals
  • clinicians documenting pediatric consultations

CareView Kids

  • parents or guardians
  • patients and families reviewing medical information

These applications may also be used by healthcare professionals and families who wish to maintain portable medical documentation.

docs.title: Nature of the software
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids function as clinical information management tools.

They allow users to:

  • record clinical observations
  • organize medical information
  • generate reports from structured data
  • exchange patient records through .pemr bundles

Some features may assist interpretation of clinical data, such as:

  • growth evaluation
  • structured summaries
  • guideline indicators
  • optional AI-generated suggestions

These features are intended only as supportive informational tools.

docs.title: Not a medical decision system
docs.text:
The software is not intended to function as an autonomous diagnostic or treatment system.

It does not provide medical diagnosis and does not replace professional clinical evaluation.

All medical decisions must remain under the responsibility of the healthcare professional.

docs.title: AI-assisted features
docs.text:
Some versions of the software may include AI-assisted clinical support features.

These features:

  • analyze structured clinical information
  • generate informational summaries or suggestions

However:

  • AI output may be incomplete or inaccurate
  • results depend on the data entered by the user
  • AI responses must always be interpreted by a qualified professional

AI features are therefore considered decision-support tools only.

docs.title: Data integrity and responsibility
docs.text:
The software includes mechanisms designed to preserve medical data, including:

  • structured databases
  • integrity verification
  • controlled bundle export and import

However, users remain responsible for:

  • verifying the accuracy of entered information
  • maintaining backups of .pemr bundles
  • handling medical records appropriately

docs.title: Limitations of the software
docs.text:
The applications are not designed to:

  • replace professional medical judgment
  • guarantee diagnostic accuracy
  • act as a regulated medical device decision engine

They are intended to support clinical documentation and record portability.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids are designed as tools for organizing and documenting pediatric medical information.

They support clinicians and families by structuring medical data and generating reports, while leaving all clinical interpretation and decision-making to qualified professionals.

docs.title: Local‑first model
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids follow a local‑first architecture.

This means that medical data is primarily stored locally on the device rather than in a central online database.

The goal of this model is to give clinicians and families direct control over medical information while reducing unnecessary exposure of sensitive health data.

docs.title: Where data is stored
docs.text:
Patient data is stored inside a .pemr bundle.

A .pemr bundle typically contains:

  • the structured medical database
  • sick and well visit records
  • growth measurements
  • attached medical documents

The bundle can be opened by:

  • CareFlow Kids (doctor app)
  • CareView Kids (patient viewer)

Because the record lives in the bundle itself, it can be moved safely between devices without relying on a permanent central server.

docs.title: No permanent central database
docs.text:
Unlike many electronic medical record systems, CareFlow Kids does not rely on a permanent centralized patient database.

Instead:

  • clinicians open or import a patient bundle when needed
  • the consultation is recorded locally
  • the updated bundle can then be exported and shared privately

This approach doesn't rely on medical data stored online. Parents are the primary owner and holders of their kids' medical data.

docs.title: Portable patient record
docs.text:
The .pemr bundle acts as a portable medical record.

This means the patient's record can move between:

  • the clinician's device
  • the family's device
  • other clinicians when required

The same bundle always contains the current cumulative medical record.

docs.title: Benefits of the local‑first approach
docs.text:
This architecture provides several advantages:

  • greater privacy — fewer centralized databases storing health data
  • patient portability — the record travels with the patient
  • offline capability — records can be accessed without internet access
  • simpler data ownership — the bundle itself represents the medical file

docs.title: Responsibilities when using local‑first records
docs.text:
Because the record is stored in a portable file, users should take reasonable precautions.

Clinicians and families should:

  • keep .pemr files in secure locations
  • share bundles only with trusted recipients
  • maintain backup copies when appropriate

These simple practices help preserve both privacy and data integrity.

docs.title: Localization
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids are designed to support multiple languages.

The goal is to allow the applications to be used in different clinical environments while keeping medical data structured and language-independent.

docs.title: Supported languages
docs.text:
The applications may provide user interface translations for multiple languages depending on the distribution version.

Examples may include:

  • English
  • French
  • Chinese
  • other languages as translations become available

The language used in the interface does not change the underlying medical data structure.

docs.title: Language-independent clinical data
docs.text:
Clinical information stored inside .pemr bundles is designed to remain language-neutral whenever possible.

For example:

  • structured tokens
  • standardized coding systems
  • structured clinical fields

This approach ensures that:

  • the same .pemr bundle can be opened in different languages
  • reports can be generated in different languages if needed
  • data remains interoperable across environments.

docs.title: Localization architecture
docs.text:
The applications use a localization-ready architecture.

This means:

  • user-visible text is separated from program logic
  • translations are stored in localization files
  • additional languages can be added without modifying core logic

This design allows the application to expand to new locales in the future.

docs.title: Reports and language
docs.text:
Visit reports generated from the application reflect the current interface language used by the clinician.

However:

  • structured medical data stored in the bundle remains unchanged
  • the same bundle can be reopened later with another language setting.

docs.title: Free text and mixed-language records
docs.text:
Some fields in the applications allow free text entry (for example clinical notes, comments, or addenda).

Free text is stored exactly as entered and is not automatically translated when the bundle is opened using another interface language.

As a result, if the same .pemr bundle is opened in applications configured with different locales, some sections may contain multiple languages.

For example:

  • clinician notes written in one language
  • parent notes written in another
  • reports generated in the interface language used at the time

In practice this situation should remain uncommon, because clinicians and families usually communicate using the same language during care interactions.

Nevertheless, the system preserves the original text exactly as written to avoid altering the clinical record.

docs.title: Future localization improvements
docs.text:
Possible future improvements may include:

  • additional interface translations
  • localized medical report templates
  • region-specific clinical terminology
  • support for additional locale settings.

docs.title: Sharing bundles safely
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids and CareView Kids use .pemr bundles to move the medical record between devices.

Because the bundle contains the complete portable patient record, it should be shared carefully.

docs.title: How bundles are usually shared
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle is typically shared between:

  • the doctor using CareFlow Kids
  • the family using CareView Kids

The normal workflow is:

  1. The doctor exports the updated .pemr bundle after a visit.
  2. The bundle is sent to the family.
  3. The family opens it in CareView Kids.
  4. The family may add notes or documents.
  5. The updated bundle can later be shared back with the doctor.

This allows the same medical record to remain cumulative and portable.

docs.title: Recommended sharing methods
docs.text:
A .pemr bundle can be shared using common file‑transfer methods such as:

  • secure messaging
  • encrypted email attachments
  • AirDrop
  • secure cloud storage links

When possible, choose a method that provides end‑to‑end encryption.

docs.title: Do not modify bundles manually
docs.text:
Although a .pemr file is technically an archive, it should not be opened or modified manually.

Manually changing the contents may:

  • corrupt the medical record
  • break file integrity
  • make the bundle unreadable by the application

Bundles should only be:

  • opened in CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids
  • updated through the application interface

docs.title: Keeping bundles secure
docs.text:
Because .pemr bundles contain sensitive medical information, they should be handled carefully.

Good practices include:

  • sharing bundles only with trusted healthcare professionals
  • avoiding public or insecure file sharing services
  • storing bundles in secure locations

Families are also encouraged to keep a backup copy of important bundles.

docs.title: Example sharing workflow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Doctor exports .pemr bundle] --> B[Bundle shared with family]
    B --> C[Opened in CareView Kids]
    C --> D[Notes or documents added]
    D --> E[Updated bundle shared back]
    E --> F[Doctor imports into CareFlow Kids]

This loop allows the patient record to remain portable while staying under the control of the clinician and family.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Fast fixes for common issues.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Bundle won’t open
  • Growth charts missing or wrong
  • Visit not showing in reports
  • Documents not appearing

docs.title: Bundle won’t open
docs.text:
If a .pemr bundle does not open in CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids, the problem is usually related to one of the following situations.

This page lists the most common causes and quick checks.

docs.title: Check that the file is a .pemr bundle
docs.text:
Make sure the file you are trying to open actually ends with:

.pemr

If the file was renamed or modified during transfer, the application may not recognize it as a valid bundle.

docs.title: Verify the bundle was transferred completely
docs.text:
If the file was sent by email, messaging, or downloaded from cloud storage, it may have been partially downloaded or corrupted.

Try the following:

  1. Delete the current copy.
  2. Download or receive the file again.
  3. Attempt to open the new copy.

docs.title: Check that the bundle was not modified manually
docs.text:
A .pemr file should not be opened or modified manually outside the application.

If the bundle was unzipped or its internal files were changed, the application may refuse to open it because:

  • file integrity checks fail
  • the internal structure was altered

If this happened, request a new export of the bundle from the doctor.

docs.title: Verify that the bundle is not outdated or incompatible
docs.text:
In rare cases, a bundle created with a much older version of the application may not open correctly.

Try:

  • updating CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids to the latest version
  • re-importing the bundle

docs.title: Integrity verification failed
docs.text:
CareFlow Kids uses integrity checks to verify that the database inside the bundle has not been altered.

If the integrity verification fails, the application may block the bundle from opening to protect the medical record.

This can happen if:

  • the file was corrupted during transfer
  • the internal files were modified

The safest solution is to obtain a fresh copy of the bundle from the original source.

docs.title: When to contact support
docs.text:
If the bundle still does not open after these checks, it may help to provide a support log.

From the application menu you can:

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Export the support log.
  3. Share it with the support team if requested.

The support log can help identify the exact cause of the issue.

docs.title: Documents not appearing
docs.text:
If documents expected in the patient record do not appear in CareFlow Kids or CareView Kids, the issue is usually related to how the bundle was updated or how the document was added.

This page lists the most common causes and quick checks.

docs.title: The document was not added through the application
docs.text:
Documents should always be added using the Documents section of the application.

If a file was manually inserted into a .pemr bundle outside the application, the system may not recognize it.

Possible solution:

  1. Open the bundle in CareFlow Kids.
  2. Go to Documents.
  3. Add the file using the Add document function.
  4. Export the updated .pemr bundle.

docs.title: The bundle was not exported after adding the document
docs.text:
If the doctor added a document but the bundle was not exported again, the family will not see the document.

Possible solution:

  1. Open the patient bundle in CareFlow Kids.
  2. Verify that the document appears in the Documents section.
  3. Export a new .pemr bundle.
  4. Share the updated bundle.

docs.title: The wrong bundle was opened
docs.text:
Families may accidentally open an older bundle that does not contain the latest documents.

Check that the most recent .pemr file is being opened.

If unsure:

  1. Delete the older bundle from the device.
  2. Import the most recent .pemr bundle again.

docs.title: Unsupported file type
docs.text:
Most common medical document formats are supported, including:

  • PDF
  • image files (such as JPG or PNG)

If a document uses an unusual format, it may not display correctly on the device.

In that case, convert the document to PDF before adding it to the record.

docs.title: When to contact support
docs.text:
If documents still do not appear after these checks, generating a support log may help diagnose the issue.

To generate a support log:

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Select Export support log.
  3. Share the log with the support team if requested.

docs.title: Growth charts missing or incorrect
docs.text:
If growth charts appear empty, incomplete, or incorrect, the issue is usually related to how measurements were recorded.

This page lists the most common causes and quick checks.

docs.title: No measurements recorded yet
docs.text:
Growth charts can only appear if measurements exist in the record.

If no measurements have been entered, the charts will remain empty.

Check that at least one measurement exists for:

  • weight
  • height or length
  • head circumference

Measurements are typically recorded during consultations or entered in the Growth Table.

docs.title: Measurements added after the visit
docs.text:
In CareFlow Kids, growth measurements are normally entered in the Growth Table before opening a visit.

If measurements were added after the visit was created, the growth analysis shown in the visit may not reflect them.

Possible solution:

  1. Open the Growth Table.
  2. Verify that the measurements are present.
  3. Reopen the visit to refresh the displayed growth information.

docs.title: Incorrect measurement units
docs.text:
Growth charts depend on correct units.

Check that measurements were entered using the expected units:

  • weight in kilograms
  • height or length in centimeters
  • head circumference in centimeters

Entering incorrect units can cause charts to display unrealistic values.

docs.title: Measurement date incorrect
docs.text:
Growth charts rely on the date of measurement to place points correctly on the timeline.

If a measurement date is wrong, the chart may appear incorrect.

Check that:

  • the measurement date corresponds to the visit date
  • the child’s date of birth is correct

docs.title: Growth chart still not appearing
docs.text:
If charts remain missing:

  • verify that the correct patient bundle is open
  • confirm that measurements exist in the growth table
  • reopen the bundle or restart the application

docs.title: When to contact support
docs.text:
If growth charts still appear incorrect after these checks, a support log may help diagnose the issue.

To generate a support log:

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Select Export support log.
  3. Share the log with support if requested.

docs.title: Visit report not appearing or incomplete
docs.text:
If a visit report does not appear or seems incomplete, the issue is usually related to how the visit was saved or recorded.

This page lists the most common causes and quick checks.

docs.title: The visit was not saved
docs.text:
Reports are generated from the saved visit data.

If the visit was not saved before closing the form, the report cannot be generated.

Possible solution:

  1. Open the patient bundle.
  2. Check the visit list.
  3. Verify that the visit appears in the list.

If the visit is missing, the consultation may need to be entered again.

docs.title: The wrong visit was selected
docs.text:
If several visits exist, it is possible to open the wrong one.

Check that:

  • the correct date is selected
  • the correct visit type (well visit or sick visit) is opened

Then open the visit again and generate the report.

docs.title: Required information missing
docs.text:
Visit reports are generated from the structured information recorded during the visit.

If key fields are empty, the report may appear incomplete.

Check that the visit includes at least:

  • visit context
  • history or reason for consultation
  • examination findings
  • problem listing
  • guidance or plan

Adding the missing information and saving the visit will update the generated report.

docs.title: The bundle was not updated
docs.text:
If the visit appears correctly in the doctor application but not in another device, the bundle may not have been updated.

Possible solution:

  1. Export a new .pemr bundle after saving the visit.
  2. Share the updated bundle.
  3. Reopen it in CareView Kids or CareFlow Kids.

docs.title: When to contact support
docs.text:
If the report still does not appear correctly after these checks, exporting a support log may help identify the problem.

To generate a support log:

  1. Open Settings.
  2. Select Export support log.
  3. Share the log with the support team if requested.

docs.title: Appendix
docs.text: Reference material for advanced users and developers.

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
• Glossary
  • Bundle structure reference
  • Assets & screenshots
